events.author: nick
events.snippet:
# Disastrous De Castro Influences

Urged by his mistress's family, King Pedro marched into Castille with a small sum of levies, sure the arrogant and
events.fullText:
# Disastrous De Castro Influences

Urged by his mistress's family, King Pedro marched into Castille with a small sum of levies, sure the arrogant and weak Castillian King would be no match for him. But instead what he found was overwhelming force, tens of thousands of men, and many allies in tow. 

The fool moved in the dead of night, and marched towards the undefended border towns, 10 thousand fighting men at his back, eager to show Iberia Alfonso’s weakness, and eager to prove himself more worthy to the crown then his father Afonso, to finally rid the stink of disgrace he had inherited.

# The Moors in Elvas

The first battle was a decisive victory, the Moorish subjects of King Alfonso XI, what manner of sorcery and diplomacy led to muslims being allowed free reign to bring terror to the good christians of the Algarve, the fiends met Portuguese steel and iron. God was with Pedro, and the heathen was crushed underfoot. The Plague bringers were left hung on posts along Seville as a warning to the encroaching Castilian army.

# The Sardinian Raids

After dispatching the Moors, rumors reached Pedro of raids on the coast of Coimbra, and he marched with haste. And what he saw truly perplexed him, a man he was sure was without friends or support had summoned allies from as far away as Sardinia. It was true Alfonso was not a man capable of fighting his own battles. At any rate they suffered a similar fate to the Moors, though Pedro could not in good conscience leave good christians to rot in the sun, and instead saw they were given final rites before burial.

# The Proud do not Endure

After months of fleeing, of begging Pedro to assault his fortifications, Alfonso finally mustered the courage to fight him on the field as he threatened Valladolid. 

From the South hundreds of banners arose from Salamanca. And they left death in their wake, thousands died on the field, along with Pedro’s ego. Alfonso led his men to victory easily, as the men of Portugal washed away like sand on a beach. With overwhelming force, Alfonso beat back the poor Portuguese levies, and captured King Pedro. Bringing a swift end to his campaign in Castille.

# And the Debts Pile

Following Pedro’s ransom, and the aftermath of the Sardinian blockade of Lisboa, the crown took on climbing amounts of debts. Pedro was forced to dismiss what little standing forces he had, forsake the upkeep of further castles mostly in Portucale, and begin seeking aid from those who had expressed interest in his rule. Though it came from the good King Edward, and the Count Joan, it became clear Pedro could never hold the nation together while beholdent to his creditors. And thus declared bankruptcy, a decision that sent shockwaves through his court that rippled throughout the countryside.

Discontent grew within every faction of the Parliament, the nobles were enraged by the forsaken outstanding debts. The common folk were enraged by the fruitless death of their kinfolk. And the clergy became critical of recent law changes, and chiefly the time Pedro spent with his mistress. The fighting men from the fields refused the call to defend the crown, the nobles sought to aid a rival claimant, and the merchants starved Lisboa in protest.

#  House Viseu

With Pedro’s reign off to such a weak start, one of Afonso’s noble bastards began navigating his fellow nobles, expanding the cracks in Pedro's reign. As he sought allies something became ever clearer, no one wanted to be ruled by a bastard, but Pedro was worse.

Following his defeat Pedro became secluded, only seeing his mistress as the wolves circled his court. 

And a wolf did appear in Joao De Viseu. A bastard of the previous king Alfonso IV, the product of an affection for a noblewoman in his youth, he had enjoyed the life of a noble and the titles of his mother. But with Pedro so far diminished he saw a chance to become more, to become King. Joao claims that Alfonso’s father had hid a legitimate marriage conducted in secret, proclaiming himself not only the legitimate child of Alfonso, but also his eldest and only legitimate one.

# The Siege of Lisboa

After gathering enough men, Joao Viseu besieged Lisboa. But with only a few members of his personal guard he was forced to flee to wherever would give him refuge. Which he would find in the court of King Erik, though his knee would never be the same.

His sons had each sought their own destinations, Joao (De Borgonha) fled to his friend King Edward, and was given command of his fleet. Afonso was given safety in his court. While Dinis fled to the County of Armagnac, to live in his wife’s childhood home with his father in-law. 

Spread across Europe each of them look homewards, wondering if they will ever return, or restore themselves.

# The New Reign

Now Joao Viseu sits on the Portuguese throne, but his enemies and rival claimants still wait, looking to see what manner of King he is. And if he will be able to hold his crown. 

While his reign is in it’s infancy, he is still at risk of those housing claimants to the throne seeing them put back in their place. Including crown Prince Juan of Castille <@422498641425596418> , Dinis in France <@460814348487753730> <@259426601865838592> , Joao and Afonso in England <@1106651545296195655> , King Pedro himself in Sweden <@326414282851876875> , and the sons of Leonor in Aragon <@1007414347347271730>. The day is won, but the morrow is not.

(I am requesting you all get claim throne cbs on portugal on behalf of the dynasty members listed, you may also do whatever you want over the midweek in regards to the situation)

events.author: Onnirio
events.snippet:
**HANSETAG** <:hansaseal:1505589926673256659> 
A new diet has been called in Lübeck, this time by the entrepreneur and consul Balthasar Türing. Though
events.fullText:
**HANSETAG** <:hansaseal:1505589926673256659> 
A new diet has been called in Lübeck, this time by the entrepreneur and consul Balthasar Türing. Though young, he has managed to open up the trade in Bordeaux, if only a bit, which means access to Biscay and the possibility of competing with the genoese at last <@170593650584059904> 

This is but a first step, for the expansion of the Hansa is paramount to ensure survival and success. Looking at the numbers, it’s clear that money is made in the west, and not the east. London & Brugge continue to be the most profitable markets, only outclassed by a Stockholm that has grown by leaps and bounds thanks to the Baltic trade (though the overall profit is minor compared to the west), and Riga keeps its steady profit flowing. <@1423762053050662983> <@326414282851876875> 

For the next expansion, With the road in Nürnburg clear, accessing the new markets in München and Wien may finally lead to Italy, the most coveted market, to compete with the Italians and demonstrate the financial prowess of the Wendish people. <@190640828954181632> <@763652399784919080> 

**The Saffron Bubble** <:Price_expensive:1345840629258780703> <:Alert_bankruptcy:1345840520789884938> 
With access to Bordeaux, the profit in London & Brugge will only increase. Especially because Türing has implemented a new financial instrument in the Hansa: commodity futures <:Burghers:1139045024890368001>. The ever-increasing demand for spice has led to the exchange of promissory notes for saffron at set prices or quantity. This has ballooned the trade of saffron, albeit artificially, as people exchange those notes like there’s not tomorrow, increasing their price even more, but there's actually not enough saffron to fulfill all of these notes...

Some have dubbed this a ‘bubble’, but Türing says they understand nothing. They, in fact, do not, and neither does Türing, but money is being made, so who cares?

events.author: Onnirio
events.snippet:
**The Southern Menace** <:V3ArmedForces:1399230393034735686> 
While the Baltic neighbours have been neutral, or even friendly, there is a growing thre
events.fullText:
**The Southern Menace** <:V3ArmedForces:1399230393034735686> 
While the Baltic neighbours have been neutral, or even friendly, there is a growing threat in the south: the duchy of Lüneburg. <@271318019052011532> The bloodthirst of the Welf dukes seems to be unstoppable, having annexed Lauenburg, Verden, conquered territories in Braunschweig, vassalized the bishopric of Hildesheim and the counties of Nienburg and Schauenburg, and conquered Stade and Harburg from the prince-bishopric of Brema. All while their neighbours struggle and suffer during the last 30 years.

The answer from the counts of Holstein and Lübeck a year ago was swift: containment. They defeated the duke and forced him to spit out Stade, Lauenburg & Harburg. This, however, has resulted in the city of Lübeck bordering bankruptcy, and pleading to the other hanseatic cities for help.

The Hansetag has concluded that a web of alliances and guarantees including the northern states must be configured. Should the dukes of Lüneburg keep their expansionism, a northern coalition will start an embargo and bring the duke to his knees by the sword if necessary. The Emperor may be missing in the north, but order is not. Tread carefully. 
-# and for the closest players <@1178339172537339909> <@210779670663135232> <@420614608399761419> (through Frisia)
-# images go 13370, 1370 (pre-Lubeck coalition), 1371 (postcoalition)

-# and <@306458253674545153> <@112960565344804864> as it involves your dioceses

With the nomination and confirmation of Albrechts rule over the Palatinate, the Nobles in Tyrol and Bavaria under his ru
events.fullText:
# THE GOLD FROM THE SOUTH # 

With the nomination and confirmation of Albrechts rule over the Palatinate, the Nobles in Tyrol and Bavaria under his rule, quickly were fully sympathetic to the struggles of the Empire. Perhaps his largest base of support emerged from the clarity of the French Crimes in Heidelberg.

Managing to convince his fellow nobles, Prince Albrecht would travel to Heidelberg with large sways of Money from his other realms: With a clear goal.

**Rebuilding Heidelberg, expand its military capabilities and prepare for the soon to come revenge against the French**

-# AI was used to generate this Picture

events.author: Guy
events.snippet:
# AND WHEN THE BEASTS ARE COMING,

# IT'S RIGHT BEFORE YOUR SIDE,

# FOR IN MY EYES, YOU'LL FIND: NO LIGHTLESS DAWNING.

*The Eretnids are slain, such
events.fullText:
# AND WHEN THE BEASTS ARE COMING,

# IT'S RIGHT BEFORE YOUR SIDE,

# FOR IN MY EYES, YOU'LL FIND: NO LIGHTLESS DAWNING.

*The Eretnids are slain, such as he had dreamed. Now, however, he imagined came the hardest part for his nation, for while secure at the moments, the tides of history had taken their course, and now that stability, and moreover potential, may yet be vanquished.*

*Relations with the Mamlukes had never been poor, per-say, but they certainly did not give comfort to Fahreen as we might've wished. However the Cicilian war reminded him starkly of threats thst his realm faced, especially as the might of the Catholic workd was unable to compete. He knew that the Mamlukes would be happy to add another vassal to their expansive collection, and he held no illusion as to what might happen should they deign it to be so. However, one nation prevented them from simply siezing what they wished within Anatolia, the Byzantines. Relations were even worse with them than the Mamlukes, being actively icy, however what Fahreen counted on (for better or worse) is them being unwilling to have all of Anatolia subjagated beneath the Mamlukes. And he held no doubt that should either make a true move against the Karamanids, he would be forced to bend the knee to the other.*

*However that left little comfort, as while he could continue is march east, if they at all deigned to work with eachother rather than let the natural greed of Empires take its course, then oncemore doom could br spelt for him. The only option, he presumed, was to aquire ways to be able, if not vanquish, then force the Empires to pay dearly for every step they took within Karaman. And while he wasn't sure he had a way to achieve that for the Mamlukes, the Romans held a far more precarious position, one that could be leveraged.*

||*The Romans had made many an enemy, a specific Northern neighbor stood out.*||

-# NOW COME TO LIFE.

events.author: GMB
events.snippet:
# THE CONSTRUCTION OF THE UNIVERSITY OF HEIDELBERG BEGINS # 

The Pope has come forward with a clear announcement that the University of Heidelberg sh
events.fullText:
# THE CONSTRUCTION OF THE UNIVERSITY OF HEIDELBERG BEGINS # 

The Pope has come forward with a clear announcement that the University of Heidelberg shall be granted a *studium generale* (a law that allows a university to teach all major fields of knowledge). This is a huge success considering how the University has not even been built yet, but receiving this legal backing will secure for the future a great degree of security for the new Plans of the Prince Palatinate Albrecht.

With this piece of legal support directly from the Papacy and of course the promises of Gold, the project may begin. Construction has now started and the people of the Empire are interested to see where this goes. 

A large argument for building this new University at the center of the Holy Roman empire is clearly the Catholic Schism. With many German Scholars studying in Paris suddenly finding themselves in a difficult political and religious situation, finding the opportunity to work instead in a more friendly environment such as Heidelberg was the perfect way forward. 

**Prince Albrecht quickly would make a speech also in front of the new construction site in Heidelberg, saying words that would echoe throughout Europe:**

`"The Pope grants us this support and let me clearly tell you all that this is a clear sign for us: a clear sign that the Papacy of Rome is the righteous one. How are we to accept these treacherous French Puppets in Lyon? Those who do not even dare to condem the actions of the vile ruler in Paris! If the scourge of Heidelberg was not evidence enough of the lack of Moral authority in Lyon and their fake Pope, then I do not know what does. We stand by Rome and the TRUE POPE!"`

(in game I will try to build the University before the actual IRL date of 1386, if not I'll request it to be added through save edits)

-# AI was used to create this picture

events.author: GMB
events.snippet:
# THE SCOURGE OF HEIDELBERG # 

Heidelberg was once a proud fortress. But against 20 thousand French troops it stood little chance. When the Keep was
events.fullText:
# THE SCOURGE OF HEIDELBERG # 

Heidelberg was once a proud fortress. But against 20 thousand French troops it stood little chance. When the Keep was laid under siege, a storming of the Castle was the clear way forward. 

In the midst of this was Countess Palatine Anna von Wittelsbach "the Prize". Although surrounded she managed to smuggle her children out of the Keep. With the aid of her husband Albrecht the line of succession was secure. The plan was for the Countess to leave too... Yet chaos struck when suddenly the storming began. 

Although her family was safe, Anna could not leave. She would as an inspirational leader rally the troops of Heidelberg to a valiant stand. But all was in vain when the 20 thousand French men took the Keep. And with this would also come the demise of Anna. 

The young lady would be brought forward to the King of France, admist the ashes of Heidelberg. When asked what to do with her the king smirked... He looked at his Savoyan wife and the decision was made. `"Bring me her Head"`

The imprisoned nobles, soldiers and onlooking peasants all gasped in shock. Pleas of Mercy were made `"She surrendered you bastards!"` some would shout in the crowds as their last breaths. And then... Anna was gone then. Quickly and without mercy she was beheaded by the French... But perhaps the most disrespectful manner was her *"Funeral"*, if you even can call it that. 

At an unknown location she would be buried, next to some random soldiers who died for Heidelberg. Or perhaps some peasant who was brave or stupid enough to raise up arms against the French occupiers. 

In there she was laid to rest, without a ceremony, without any respect... Only with the King of France Philippe VII de Valois smirking... 

The Prize would as many other treasures be buried in the ground... Hidden for ever... Never to be found again.

<@460814348487753730>

events.author: ST Peter
events.snippet:
# The Death of King Guy II de Lusignan
**The Crusader King. Conqueror of Cilicia...and loser of Cilicia.**

When Guy ascended to the thrones of Cyprus
events.fullText:
# The Death of King Guy II de Lusignan
**The Crusader King. Conqueror of Cilicia...and loser of Cilicia.**

When Guy ascended to the thrones of Cyprus and Jerusalem in 1348, the country was stagnant. Hugh IV had ruled for over two decades, but after the failure of the "crusade" of 1336, he was stuck in the mire in regards of what to do next. Guy changed that. In his twenty-two years of rule he made it an exciting reign to witness. He abandoned his father's policy of trying desperately to acquire the aid of the French Crown and instead took it upon himself to gather a new coalition, which he might use on the mainland. It took a decade of diplomacy, but Guy was eventually able to gather allies for his invasion of Cilicia in 1358, which he claimed the crown of. Some of Guy's newfound friends would provide soldiers for the expedition, others, like Genoa and the Papacy, would provide the coin needed to fund it.

The campaign was bloody, but short. With an army composed of his own forces, the Knight's Hospitaller, and a contingent of the Imperial Army of the Empire of Constantinople, the battles against the Armenian King went back and forth for a few months, until the dam broke and the combined armies cut deep into the Cilician heartlands. With the fall of Sis in late 1358, the resistance crumbled, and Guy would officially be coronated as King Guy I of Cilicia in January 1359. In the aftermath, Guy would go about integrating this new territory, rewarding the Armenian nobles whom supported his claim, uplifting the authority of the Roman Church over the Armenian Church, and applying Frankish law to the conquered region. 

The result of all this was a relatively autonomous Kingdom of Cilicia, where the Armenian nobles retained much of their previous control over the government and serfs. Guy had little illusions about being able to maintain any decent amount of direct, royal control over Cilicia. This would do.

Even with the autonomy the Armenian nobles had under the feudal system of the Franks, Guy was still able to profit immensely from the prosperous cities and towns there. It was a triumph the likes of which Guy would never again surpass. For the first time since the fall of Acre and the complete loss of the Holy Lands, there was confidence and a focused foreign policy which was evidently able to bring results. New flags were soon sewn for the Lusignans in recognition of this glory, displaying the coat of arms of the Kingdom of Cilicia alongside those of the Kingdoms of Cyprus and Jerusalem. 

For a few short years following the conquest there was peace, and by the mercy of God it was a fine peace indeed. With the wealth of Cilician cities flowing partly into the Lusignan treasury, it was a time of wealth that the Kings of Cyprus and Jerusalem had never seen before. Several building projects would result from this wealth, including the constructing of a new, glorious Royal Court, and a gothic style Roman-Catholic Cathedral in Morphou. Though not particularly large or intricate, it was still a remarkable copy of other Cathedrals in the west. As the years passed, however, that prosperous peace, that renewed confidence and focus would all quickly be thrown unwillingly away, in a blaze of death and defeat. 

--
Guy had worked hard to secure his new Kingdom, the only one he had that was vulnerable to an invasion overland. With the Armenian nobles eventually placated and thus safe from a revolt, Guy sent out diplomats to several of the Turkish Beyliks which were the de facto rulers of the Turkish raiding bands which in truth rarely listened to their overlords and simply raided neighboring states whenever they had the inclination. In this effort Guy found Fahreddin Ahmed Bey, ruler of the Karamanids, a worthy friend. In exchange for a tribute, the Karamanids would attempt to rein in their Turkish raiders from attacking into Cilicia.

Of course, this effort didn't always prevent Turkish raids into Guy's new Kingdom, but it did help lessen the frequency of the Turkish incursions, and it left Guy feeling mostly satisfied that he'd done what he could to secure western Cilicia. The east however, was a different matter. The Lusignan Kingdoms and the Mamluk Sultanate had been raiding each other for decades. At times it cooled and the raids weren't frequent, other times it picked up in intensity, but it was always an unbalanced back and forth. Guy expected this informal contest to continue after his conquest of Cilicia. That was a mistake.

The Mamluks had clearly been eyeing Cilicia for many years, for they amassed a large army, far above anything the Catholic states in the east could raise. With the Mamluk forces continuing to gather, Guy found that Christendom seemed to be embroiled by other matters in the west, distracted and uncaring of the situation in the east. And so even before the first of the tens of thousands of Mamluk soldiers crossed the border in 1367, the prospect of victory seemed distant. Matters were only made worse when the Emperor of Constantinople, a place Guy had visited in his youth and retained close ties with, John V himself even having visited Cyprus not ten years before in an act of friendship, decided not to provide any aid whatsoever to the Lusignans.

Betrayal stings, but that did not mean that Guy would give up the Kingdom of Cilicia without a fight. There were still some connections that remained firm. The Knights Hospitaller rushed to the Lusignan's aid, as did the Genoese, whose influence and trading power in the Kingdom continued to grow rapidly. Still, the combined forces of the Lusignans, the Knights, and the Genoese could not hope to put up a direct fight, for even though they numbered in the thousands it could not compare to the size of the forces opposed to them.

Before the Mamluk host could concentrate its forces, the Catholic army scored some early victories, killing or maiming some minor thousands in the mountains of eastern Cilicia. Direct confrontation with the Mamluk army would only lead to certain defeat, so the war councils between the allies developed a strategy of delay and attrition. This ensured the conflicts longevity, and it was Guy's hope that after enough of the enemies had starved or died of disease the invaders might retreat or be willing to negotiate.

For over three years the conflict dragged on in Cilicia. The strategy of the Catholics meant that the conquest was slow, but one by one the cities slowly fell. Tarsus put up an exceptionally tough fight, its garrison holding out for well over a year and a half, but the sheer numbers of the Mamluk host put aside any opportunity to relieve the siege. Furthermore, the Sultan dismissed all of Guy's attempts to negotiate a peace. In 1370, there was simply nothing left to do but withdraw the allied forces back to Cyprus, where they would finally be able to rest and resupply without the threat of attack. 

Guy was distraught when he returned to Cyprus with the army. The culmination of a decade of diplomacy and careful preparations was already lost. It must've been at that moment that he knew how his father felt after the failure of 1336. It seemed as if the Kingdom of Cilicia was to be nothing more than the Kingdom of Jerusalem. A purely titular title, with no lands or power to come with it. At the very least, Guy would not have to stew with his failure for long. His health had been declining ever since he led the army in its defense of his new Kingdom. He was already over 50 by that point, and military campaigns rarely see one's health get stronger. An outbreak of influenza on Cyprus was to be the nail in his coffin, for he caught the disease in late 1371, and he would die not long after, on Novemeber 26th.

---
The Kingdom of Cyprus is now almost right back to where it was when Guy was coronated. It's a little richer, sure, but the Kingdom's fate...its very existence, stands in limbo. The conquest of Cilicia had finally given the Kingdom a purpose again, a will of its own beyond merely being a spectator to events, a state devoid of its agency and identity which were lost when Acre fell. The loss of Cilicia has meant the loss of that renewed purpose as well. The Kingdom of the Lusignans now floats adrift oncemore, like a boat over an endless sea, set without any course or destination...merely allowing the tides to take them wherever it wants to go, whose demoralized passengers wonder why they're even there at all.

So with the death of Guy, it falls to yet another Lusignan to try to find some purpose here in the abandoned Catholic east. King Peter I, eldest son of Guy, now has that responsibility. At just sixteen years old, he is inexperienced and yet unknown in the greater world, thus meaning he has no connections to draw on. His marriage to a Valois may mean a return to seeking French aid, much like his grandfather tried. But, the young are often foolish, and who is to say how Peter will turn out as King? In any case, he received the crown of Cyprus in Nicosie, and then the Crown of Cilicia in Kyrenia. He now travels to Famagusta to receive the crown of Jerusalem. Three coronations for only one real Kingdom. It is a sad state of affairs no doubt.

So, the stage is set for Famagusta, and the beginning of a new, uncertain reign on Cyprus...

-# (Left to Right) The Death of King Guy II, coin of King Peter I, the coat of arms of the Lusignan Kingdoms of Cyprus, Cilicia, and Jerusalem.

events.author: DerNette
events.snippet:
# **In Memory of Raoul d'Alsace**

Duke Raoul d'Alsace of Lorraine was called to rule at only seventeen years of age following the untimely death of h
events.fullText:
# **In Memory of Raoul d'Alsace**

Duke Raoul d'Alsace of Lorraine was called to rule at only seventeen years of age following the untimely death of his father. Forced to learn much on his own, he nevertheless proved himself a capable ruler and took important steps toward his greatest ambition: the reunification of Metz with Lorraine.
Throughout his reign, Raoul worked tirelessly to bring order and stability to the region. He invested heavily in infrastructure, expanded resource production, and sought to strengthen the prosperity of his people. He honored his promises to the city of Metz through the establishment of a market and oversaw the growth of his seat at Nancy through the development of a new town.
Yet perhaps his greatest strength was his military skill. Through numerous campaigns and battles, he gained considerable experience as a commander and quickly earned a reputation as a respected leader among his peers.
When the Empire called upon its princes to join the war for the liberation of Flanders from France, Raoul remained loyal to his Emperor and answered that call without hesitation. It was a decision that ultimately cost him his life, as it did for many sons of Lorraine.

Now his eldest son, Tombo d'Alsace, assumes the ducal mantle. Having already served in the administration of the Pays de Messin, he is no stranger to the responsibilities of governance. He now inherits a duchy scarred by war, following the occupation and devastation brought by the conflict with France. Thousands have fled their homes, and much of Lorraine faces a long road to recovery.
The task before the new Duke is clear: to rebuild, restore, and ensure that the sacrifices and achievements of his father are not forgotten.

**In memory of Raoul d'Alsace**
*19. August 1370*

events.author: DerNette
events.snippet:
**Many Dead, Many Displaced**

The goal of the war was the liberation of Flanders from France, yet the greatest battlefield ultimately became Lorraine
events.fullText:
**Many Dead, Many Displaced**

The goal of the war was the liberation of Flanders from France, yet the greatest battlefield ultimately became Lorraine itself. Not only did 4,000 proud Lorrainers give their lives in the conflict, but an Austrian army of 13,000 men was likewise destroyed upon our soil.
As the war spread across the region and Metz fell to enemy forces, fear swept through the duchy. Thousands fled their homes into neighboring lands in search of safety. While many were spared the horrors of war through their flight, Lorraine now faces a significant loss of population. It is a wound that will not heal quickly and will leave a lasting scar upon the history of our duchy.
In response to this crisis, the Parliament of Lorraine has already approved the first measures for recovery. Investments are being directed toward food production, the expansion of farms, and the restoration of local supplies to ensure that the people of Lorraine may once again find security and stability.
The events of the war have also demonstrated the importance of maintaining our own market and regional commerce. Without these foundations, the consequences of the conflict and our dependence upon foreign trade would have been far more severe.
Our foremost objective is to encourage the return of those who fled the war and to provide new opportunities for the people who remain. The rebuilding of Lorraine shall not only restore what has been lost, but also create opportunities for farmers, craftsmen, and families to prosper once more.
Further reconstruction projects are planned in the years ahead to restore prosperity, strengthen stability, and ensure that Lorraine recovers from the hardships it has endured.

**Tombo d'Alsace**
**Duke of Lorraine, 1371**

events.author: Dods
events.snippet:
# A sermon for the faithful dead

While usually there would be disputes over the presence of the Arch-Bishop entering the city of Köln, in such times
events.fullText:
# A sermon for the faithful dead

While usually there would be disputes over the presence of the Arch-Bishop entering the city of Köln, in such times after great loss there was little resistance from the cities council, residents cry out "Murderer" and "Butcher" for the complete massacre at Dordrecht, Andreas entourage have to keep close for the safety of the Prince-Bishop.

Hosting a sermon in remembrance for those who fell in the war against the French in Köln Cathedral, the air in the cathedral is off a solemn respite.

"I thank all of you for attending here today, we are deeply wounded by those who we've lost so recently, those who had the courage and selflessness to give the ultimate sacrifice in the name of the lord.

It is unfortunate that those in gods grace shall be put through such test when the sinful desire to corrupt the flock, but with your faith and dedication. The sinful shall face the lords retribution for it is you who are true to the church, the lord and his kingdom.

Those who have given so much will be in the heavens above, being rewarded with the tranquillity they deserve so much for being such faithful servants, it is our duty as gods guided children to carry the flame they have passed to us and hold the bastion of the one true church.

For those that cannot fight for our salvation, our justice, pray for those who carry the burden, pray for those who are afflicted by such corruption that we must save, pray to the lord and he shall receive you.

Amen"

After the sermon is done, Prince-Bishop Andreas communes with some of the residents of Köln to grant some peace to those who have lost loved ones.

Both Count Immanuel (<@792904515795550218>) and Duke Friedrich II (<@420614608399761419>/<@190640828954181632>) receive invites to the sermon.

events.author: tintock
events.snippet:
## Renewing the Römerbrücke. A Bridge across the Moselle 

In the year of our lord 1359 the Burghers of the recovering town of Trier laid the first st
events.fullText:
## Renewing the Römerbrücke. A Bridge across the Moselle 

In the year of our lord 1359 the Burghers of the recovering town of Trier laid the first stone on a mighty project. Renewing the ancient Roman bridge across the Moselle. 
The Ancient bridge called locally the [Römerbrücke](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roman_Bridge_(Trier)) or simply the "Roman Bridge", as it was first built by the Romans in the 2nd Century AD. Is an important connection along the Moselle that promotes both trade and movement in the local area.

The great bridge fell out of use during the dire years of the plague, during the disuse two of the nine original Roman pillars started to collapse, prompting the local population to stop using the bridge and to start using a poorly run local ferry instead. But with the renewed investment from both the Cathedral Chapter of Trier and the Burghers of the Town, the old 2nd century pillars are being rebuilt and the bridge is soon to be reopened.
-# (repost for Map Bot)

events.author: Wolf legion 45
events.snippet:
**A Clandestine meeting:**

During a particularly dark night in Palemmu, Sicily, a number of men in non-descript clothes walk into a shady bar. They w
events.fullText:
**A Clandestine meeting:**

During a particularly dark night in Palemmu, Sicily, a number of men in non-descript clothes walk into a shady bar. They walk to the corner were a group of men sit watching with drinks, their leader a smiling man with a large moustache and a curved sword at his side. The men sit down at the table and stare at the moustached men who only smiles. His gaze drifts down to the folds of their clothes were a small cross can be seen half hidden. His smile only gets larger as he leans in "So what can we do for you today?"

The man with the cross looks at the man seriously a moment before replying "We have a job for you, one im sure you'll enjoy"

The moustache man cocks his head "Oh? And is this for your boss or yourself?" he smiles a knowing smile.

The cross man hesitates before continuing "My... boss, as you put it."

The moustache man nods "I see, and what can we do for 'His Holyness' today?" He says the title with mockery but no small amount of seriousness. 

If the cross man notices he gives no indication "He wishes your Sultan to raid his enemies, Specifically the Imperial pretenders in the east."

The Moustache man gives a serious look, "And he would want us to raid and take their people for what? His blessing" It is not a serious jab but the question is hidden in it

The cross man notices and puts a large bag onto the table, the sound of it hitting is loud with the sound of coins. 

As he looks in the moustache man smiles again "Well lads, looks like we will be raiding an empire." As he holds his hand out to shake, which after a moment of hesitation the cross man does.

Word is sent to both their respective leaders, and the pact is struck.

<@150411715757080576> <@302128861209165824> <@422498641425596418> <@297185126947880960>

events.snippet:
# The Second Cretan Revolt
The island of Crete has long been one of the most valuable possessions of the Republic. Positioned at the crossroads of the
events.fullText:
# The Second Cretan Revolt
The island of Crete has long been one of the most valuable possessions of the Republic. Positioned at the crossroads of the eastern Mediterranean, it serves as both a vital trading station and an important link between Venice and her holdings abroad. Yet the island has not always been peaceful. The recent revolt by elements of the local nobility serves as a reminder that the integration of Crete into the Venetian sphere remains an ongoing process. Grievances over administration, taxation, and local privileges have periodically led sections of the population to challenge Venetian authority.

The Second Cretan Revolt was not merely a local disturbance. It tested the Republic's ability to maintain order across its maritime domains and demonstrated the importance of strong governance throughout the *Stato da Mar*. The swift suppression of the revolt has restored order to the island. Crete is too important to be neglected, and the security of Venetian trade in the eastern Mediterranean depends upon the continued stability of the island.

events.author: Rafaam
events.snippet:
**The Enemy of My Enemy is My Friend**

It is often said that no fraternity is possible between a Muslim and a Christian. And especially, one would im
events.fullText:
**The Enemy of My Enemy is My Friend**

It is often said that no fraternity is possible between a Muslim and a Christian. And especially, one would imagine that the Great Roman Empire of the East would never align itself with the interests of those south of the Mediterranean. Those who had only recently been proclaimed savages and pirates have now been received as allies in a world that did not welcome the Empire of the East.

An improbable alliance has been forged between Tunis and the Empire of the East. A Muslim and an Orthodox standing together against a threat to them both. The Papacy has long overextended its reach among many Christian rulers and peoples, but nowhere has this been seen as clearly as in Byzantium. Its ruler and its people have watched in horror as the so-called "holy" servants of the madman in Rome forcibly converted their Orthodox brothers in Wallachia and Bulgaria.

Neither the people of Tunis nor those of Byzantium were eager to fight alongside one another, but when the orators marched through the streets of Constantinople and announced that the Papacy had placed an active reward of no less than 1,000 gold pieces for the capture of Constantinople, the people of the Queen of Cities set aside their concerns regarding cooperation with the Muslims.

Without even realizing it, the many Catholic peoples and rulers who had antagonized the Empire for so long had themselves pushed the Emperor and his people toward accepting such an alliance. 

What was even more surprising than the existence of the alliance, is the fact, that both peoples have returned home victorious. A century ago, one would have shuddered at the mere thought of such a victory, achieved by Muslim and Christian alike. Yet it is now the reality of history, and only time will tell how it will shape the future of these great peoples and those around them.

<@297185126947880960>

events.author: Rafaam
events.snippet:
**Two Cities, One Spirit**

Both Constantinople and Messina stood in danger.

One, Messina, now under a new master, had recently come under the contro
events.fullText:
**Two Cities, One Spirit**

Both Constantinople and Messina stood in danger.

One, Messina, now under a new master, had recently come under the control of Tunis along with the island of Sicily, taken from Naples. The Roman Empire and Tunis had joined forces in mutual defense. By what many could only call a miracle, they succeeded, and the siege of Messina was lifted.

The other was Constantinople itself, which had already suffered greatly from Moroccan pirate raids financed by the Pope and now faced the threat of devastation brought about by the ambitions of the Holy See.

As both cities survived the crisis, their peoples proclaimed in great numbers that only divine intervention had allowed them to endure. Many gave thanks for the salvation of their homes in Messina, while in Constantinople prayers were offered for the safe return of the Byzantine fishermen and sailors who had embarked upon what seemed an impossible journey.

The Emperor, wishing to preserve this spirit of unity for longer than a single generation, has prepared a delegation of clergy and architects to travel to Messina and establish an Orthodox monastery within its borders. Its purpose shall be to remind future generations that coexistence between our peoples and our faiths is not only possible, but achievable.

The Queen of Cities likewise extends an invitation to a similar delegation from Tunis, should Sultan Abu Bakr III Hafsid deem such a construction appropriate.

<@297185126947880960> <@327561459976372224>

events.author: Rafaam
events.snippet:
**The true miracle of the XIVth century**

At the very beginning of the war between the defenders of Sicily, the Great Roman Empire of the East and th
events.fullText:
**The true miracle of the XIVth century**

At the very beginning of the war between the defenders of Sicily, the Great Roman Empire of the East and the Hafsid Sultanate of Tunis. Fighting against the invading Italian warmongers under the influence of the *Madman in Rome*, alongside their ignorant Iberian supporters, the fleets of both Tunis and Byzantium were lost, as if by some infernal hand intervening in the battle.

Yet the spirit of the defenders was not so easily broken.

The fishermen of Byzantium, initially reserved for precisely such an emergency, set out across the Mediterranean just before the enemy fleets succeeded in blockading the entrance to the Aegean Sea.

Under the cover of chaos brought by war, they made their way toward the lands of the Sultanate. Fear accompanied them, for all communication with the Empire had been severed, and their only hope was to reach any Tunisian forces before they were discovered or destroyed.

And, as if by a miracle, they did indeed find one of the nearby armies preparing to embark on its own transport ships. Yet that embarkation never took place. As they were all destroyed.

Thus, simple Byzantine fishermen became the sole hope of the great Sultanate army crossing the strait to Sicily to defend it from the invaders.

Many sailors did not dare to believe that even a single crossing to the island could be completed successfully, yet they were proven wrong. What neither of the imperial navies could accomplish, a humble collection of fishermen and merchants from the Empire achieved with ease. It was as though the Lord Himself had concealed them from the sight of their enemies.

As such, they transported the entire army across the sea, enduring multiple crossings of the strait, and not a single man was lost.

<@297185126947880960> <@234699731715096576> <@1007414347347271730> <@422498641425596418>

events.author: Guy
events.snippet:
# DO YOU WANT IT, REALLY REALLY WANT IT?

*In 1260, the last remnants of the Sejuks in Rum were crushed.*

*It was a long process, spanning a few hund
events.fullText:
# DO YOU WANT IT, REALLY REALLY WANT IT?

*In 1260, the last remnants of the Sejuks in Rum were crushed.*

*It was a long process, spanning a few hundred year, but by the end of it, their exited only scattered remnants of what they once held, a poor echo at best.*

*Their once mighty Empire, shattered by Crusade, Incompetency, and the ever encroaching Mongol Horde. The alliance they had made, with the combined efforts Catholic, Orthodox, and Muslim realms, were all shattered before the Mongol Hordes. In it's aftermath, the land was divided unto each of the many Oghuz tribes whom inhabited the former Empire, forming the Modern Beyliks that have defined politics within 14th century Anatolia.*

*That idea, of a great Turkish Empire, spanning from the endless mountains of Persia to the crystalline waters of Aegean, didn't die with it's fall. Cracked, perhaps, it's veneer of confidence shattered by the endless pressures of the world, yet, not all things lost are truly gone. Just on the border of the Turkic tribes, the Romans, their fate once thought concluded to be a dying spark, have risen as the Purple Phoenix a fraction of their former glory.*

*So why should this idea, the privilege, of rising once more be kept away from the Turks? However, Empires do not rise in a day, nor do they even have prominence until their very zenith. It takes time, effort and dedication, and moreover and perhaps most importantly, luck. Work has begun of course, the rising Karamanids carry the flame of the idea made by the Seljuks... and yet. It is incomplete, and there is much to be done. Not just in a sense of simple conquest, but the very foundation of empire themselves are still being laid. Mint, coin food, all are slowly being given their fair share of due diligence, yet such a process is slow, and will take much time and effort.*

-# WILL YOU TAKE IT, COME ALONG AND TAKE IT?

events.author: Guy
events.snippet:
# WANNA FAKE IT, HIDE YOUR OWN AND FAKE IT?

*The backbone of Empires is not great men seeking to satisfy their own ambition, nor is it the weapons an
events.fullText:
# WANNA FAKE IT, HIDE YOUR OWN AND FAKE IT?

*The backbone of Empires is not great men seeking to satisfy their own ambition, nor is it the weapons and armaments that carry it to victory in battle, but rather it is the very coin that defines it.*

*The economic base of the Karamanids is... les than satisfactory per say. The entire wealth of the nation is still less than the taxes extracted the Byzantine Empire alone, and not only that, but the Bey is able to exercise VERY little control over a majority of territories that have been recently siezed. However, that has not deterred Fahreen, for while he may be lackluster at both diplomacy and military affairs, he is no slouch when it comes to administration of nations.*

*he has, in his reign, successfully created what he has dubbed the 'Geribesleme' system, wherein in exchange for an extreme increase of privleges, the Nascent Burghur and Nobility classes have been forced unto contracts wherein they construct a majority of roads for the Beylik. The has allowed for the first semblance of connections within the Empire, with Sivas, Konya, and the Seljuk mint all connected by a single line of road. This in turn has allowed the Bey to more effectively exploit the few generators of profit within his territories, and with the securing of his line of Silk road, the Fahreen is establishing near complete control of trade in the region for the merchants of Karaman.*

*This is combined with the recent production, and subsequent constructions of Bog Iron and tool guilds, along with plans for weapons workshops, within cities and the rural countrysides will allow for the Karamanids economy to comparatively surge as to where it was before.*

*It is a slowly and steady process, but so long as a sort of peace is maintained, wealth shall flow unto Anatolia, and not just within the Romans.*

-# OH, IT'D TAUNT YA UNTIL THE DAY YOU BREAK IT!

events.author: tintock
events.snippet:
# The Western Schism, the end of Clerical Celibacy?

[With the Kingdom of France rejecting the Pope in Rome](https://discord.com/channels/507915886477
events.fullText:
# The Western Schism, the end of Clerical Celibacy?

[With the Kingdom of France rejecting the Pope in Rome](https://discord.com/channels/507915886477312023/1505545415213449216/1510747164794683563), an erosion in Church authority is taking place. An erosion in authority that is allowing the very members of the Church to fall to their own carnal desires.

The Church mandates that its Bishops must practice celibacy, in 1074 the great pope Gregory VII published a Encyclical that[ *absolved those obedience to bishops who allowed married priests*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gregorian_Reform#Clerical_celibacy). While this reform faced resistance the sheer weight of the imperial Gregorian style papacy, the papacy that could not err forced the reform to find some success. For a time it became rarer for Bishops and Priests to father children. Especially those Bishops and Priests in **visible** positions.

Now in the later half of the 14th century, with the waning power of Church authority. The ugly practice of Bishops fathering Children is returning, and many prominent Imperial Bishops are not only engaging in the practice, but promoting it.

**Prince-Bishop Leopold von Habsburg of Trier** is one of many imperial Bishops that have taken the weakness of church authority to fulfill their own carnal and earthly desires to father children. Leopold at 40 years of age took a local Moselle Noble of 18 years as his mistress, with this lady he has fathered a son, not a bastard son, but a legitimate son **Franz Friedrich von Habsburg**. Leopold is not the first Prince-Bishop to take a mistress and father "legitimate" children, the practice has been ongoing in the more minor imperial Bishoprics since before the plague. But Prince-Bishop Leopold **with his colleague in Mainz** are the two most prominent members of the Clergy to partake in the sinful practice.

These two men, the Prince-Bishop of Trier and the Prince-Bishop of Mainz. Two of the most prominent members of the Clergy, two Archbishops of the Empire, **two supporters of the Pope in Rome**, have both taken mistresses and fathered Children. If the Pope in Rome cannot keep two of his most prominent supports in line and following the rule of clerical law then what evidence does the rest of Christendom have that the Pope in Rome has the moral authority to be the Vicar of Christ?

Why would Christendom not instead look to Paris, the ascendant French Monarchs and his loyal Cardinals to lead them in prayer? 

[The Lay Clergy of Trier and the Bishop of Trier have often been at odds with one another, with the Lay Clergy being much more spiritual and the Bishop more interested in world individualistic concerns](https://discord.com/channels/507915886477312023/1434628920371581079/1505384895822823564). Now with the Bishop himself taking a noble Mistress and fathering a legitimate child these differences are growing further. Outright hostility may even be inevitable. The ideas of Reform and enforced rules for Priests, or Bishops alike are being starting to be discussed in the more desperate parts of Trier.

## Amongst many of the lay clergy in Trier the call for Papal reform grows, whether that reform comes from Paris or from Rome has yet to be decided.

events.author: JPThunda
events.snippet:
**The Power of Coin & Commerce**


For many years now the Burghers, the merchant class, had steadily increased their power and influence within the Tu
events.fullText:
**The Power of Coin & Commerce**


For many years now the Burghers, the merchant class, had steadily increased their power and influence within the Tunisian court.  Given the nature of the Tunisian state, that of a mercantile power, heavily dependent on trade at the heart of the Mediterranean, none of this was terribly surprising.  It was early into the reign of **Sultan Abu Bakr Hafsid III** that this change was finally acknowledged in a number of parliamentary reforms which saw the Burgers granted formal representation within the assembly.  This would no doubt have impacts for years to come…

events.author: JPThunda
events.snippet:
**Prelude: Upon these Walls…**


War was coming to Tunis, and only a fool would think otherwise.

Despite every effort on the part of the Tunisian dip
events.fullText:
**Prelude: Upon these Walls…**


War was coming to Tunis, and only a fool would think otherwise.

Despite every effort on the part of the Tunisian diplomats and representatives, the pontiff in Rome had made it his personal mission to wrest Sicily from the Sultanate’s grasp.  Just because it was his wish, though, did not mean that the Tunisians would make it easy.

The young Sultan charged his uncle, **Ibrahim Abu Ishaq Hafsid**, with securing the defenses in Messina.  Ibrahim had a reputation as being quite **stubborn**, and it was reasoned that he would be exacting in overseeing the defenses of the city.  Funds were allocated, time tables were laid out...and exacting, Ibrahim was.

Under Ibrahim’s governorship Messina became an impregnable fortress, with the construction of high, modern stone walls to protect the city proper and its harbor as well as the construction of an *Agadir*--a fortified granary–to further supplement the food stores of the fortress.

With these preparations it was reasoned that the city could hold out nigh-indefinitely.

It would have to.

<@302128861209165824> <@234699731715096576> <@1007414347347271730> <@327561459976372224> <@874750350538727434> ||Beginnings of my posts delving into the war.||

events.author: JPThunda
events.snippet:
**The Crusade for Sicily - The Battle of the Gulf of Tunis**


The war began in July, 1368.

Aragonese and Castilian fleets had been spotted plying th
events.fullText:
**The Crusade for Sicily - The Battle of the Gulf of Tunis**


The war began in July, 1368.

Aragonese and Castilian fleets had been spotted plying the seas around Sicily for some time, and a formal declaration of hostilities came in July.  The very first act of the war was a heavy blockade of Tunis by the amassed fleets of the Crusaders.  Although the Roman warships had arrived in Tunis months before the outset of hostilities, the Tunisian fleet was still outnumbered nearly 2 to 1.  Despite this, the Tunisian admiralty reasoned that their fleets were of a higher quality, and could perhaps prevail, or at least inflict pyrrhic losses.  This assessment, however, was horribly flawed.

The battle took place in the Gulf of Tunis and it was decisive, with nearly the entire combat strength of the Roman and Tunisian fleets wiped out.  While this was a major blow to the defenders, it would ultimately prove to have only limited impact on the outcome of the war.  Whether this early success led to a complacent hubris on the seas, or whether the events that followed were entirely the product of Tunisian and Byzantine cunning, who can say?  All that can be said is this; following the battle in the Gulf of Tunis the Crusader fleet settled into a blockade focused at Messina to support a Neapolitan landing.  Despite overwhelming naval superiority, they did not, however, engage in a complete blockade of the island.  This would ultimately prove to be a fatal mistake.

events.author: JPThunda
events.snippet:
**Running the Blockade**


At the start of the war, some 24,000 Tunisian soldiers mustered in North Africa, while only 150 levies and 4,400 Sicilian l
events.fullText:
**Running the Blockade**


At the start of the war, some 24,000 Tunisian soldiers mustered in North Africa, while only 150 levies and 4,400 Sicilian levies arose in Sicily.  This presented a problem, as with the loss of combat ships, any landing could be effectively disrupted by a blockade, and there was little the defenders could do about it.  Moving quickly, the Sultan ordered his forces divided, with half making for Libya while the other half marched for Annabah to rendezvous with a fleet of Tunisian fishing vessels that would ferry them to Sicily.  Some 6,000 of the 12,000 present boarded the ships and made for Trapani.

In a profound run of bad luck, however, a roving Sardinian fleet stumbled across the unguarded Tunisian transports.  Several of the ships were sunk, taking 2,000 men with them and forcing the Tunisian fleet to withdraw.  The Sardinians did not realize the significance of this stroke of luck, however, and rather than blockading the Tunisian transports, they returned to Cagliari.  This allowed the Tunisian navy to make a second, successful run, delivering 4,000 men to the island.  Already 18,000 Neapolitans had besieged Messina, and with only 8,400 men on the island breaking the siege would be a tall order indeed, however these would not be the only Tunisian soldiers to make their way to Sicily, as the Roman fishing vessels managed to overshadow the Tunisian blockade running in spectacular fashion.

events.author: JPThunda
events.snippet:
**The Byzantine Gambit**


An early contingency within the war planning called for the Roman transport fleet to assist in ferrying Tunisian soldiers t
events.fullText:
**The Byzantine Gambit**


An early contingency within the war planning called for the Roman transport fleet to assist in ferrying Tunisian soldiers to the island of Sicily, but with the disaster in the Tunisian Gulf, the importance of this effort ballooned.  After the first successful Tunisian run of the blockade, the Tunisian vessels were blockaded at Trapani, and so now the success or failure of the entire campaign rested squarely upon Roman shoulders.

Despite being far from the island of Sicily, the Libyan coastline was chosen as the site of this operation for one, key reason.  It was a blind spot for the Catholics.  It was from Libya that the daring marines had managed to take Messina during the first Neapolitan invasion, and this was no coincidence.  Due to the distance from the major population centers on the island and the relatively low population, the southeast coast of Sicily held little significance, and so the sea lanes were unguarded.  What was more, Roman vassals on Malta could relay any information about fleet movements and safeguard against a Crusader ambush.  It created a vital, clear shipping lane, and it was this lane that the Roman navy would exploit.

The first landing carried 10,000 Tunisians, including much of the tribal cavalry.  This force, if intercepted and defeated, would ensure the Tunisian defeat and have a lasting impact on the ability of the Sultanate to defend itself, but thanks to the skill of the Roman admirals all 10,000 men landed on Sicily completely uncontested.

This miracle was repeated time and time again and, in total, 16,000 Tunisians and 4,000 Mamluk soldiers were ferried to the island of Sicily right under the noses of the Crusader fleet.

The sheer scale and impact of this effort cannot be understated, as it gave the Tunisian forces a total of 28,500 soldiers on the island, accounting for Sicilians and Mamluks, resulting in a local superiority of forces on the island.  Combined with the prepared positions, this was a huge advantage for the defenders that would prove insurmountable.  The blunder by the Crusaders in allowing these forces onto the island would prove to be the decisive factor in the outcome of war.

<@302128861209165824>

events.author: JPThunda
events.snippet:
**The Battle of Messina**


The first major land battle would occur at Messina, where some 22,000-25,000 Neapolitan soldiers had besieged the fortress
events.fullText:
**The Battle of Messina**


The first major land battle would occur at Messina, where some 22,000-25,000 Neapolitan soldiers had besieged the fortress since the earliest days of the war.  Ibrahim Abu Ishaq Hafsid held the city, and despite the mighty defences and stalwart garrison it could not truly hold indefinitely.  Supplies had not yet reached a critical level, but it was far better for it never to get that far at all.

The Tunisian army under the command of **al-Fadl Hafsid**, brother of Ibrahim, numbered some 14,000 at this time, and it was judged more than equal to the task of repelling the Neapolitans.  The 4,500 Sicilian forces were patrolling the island’s northern coast, near Palermo, and would play no part in the battle.

al-Fadl’s army joined with the garrison in sallying against the Neapolitan invaders and, thanks to the mighty defenses and earthworks that Ibrahim had constructed within the harsh terrain, it seriously limited the ability of the Neapolitans to maneuver and engage.  Before the walls of the city the Neapolitan army was routed and repulsed, forced to flee back across the straits into Italy.

Although it was hoped that this would herald the end of the conflict, the Crusaders had one more card yet to play, and as the Roman navy ferried more and more troops to the island, the Tunisians took stock, prepared, and waited.

events.author: JPThunda
events.snippet:
**The Second Landings - The Battle of Palermo**


Imitation, it is said, is the greatest form of flattery, and had Sultan Umar Abu Hafs Hafsid II been
events.fullText:
**The Second Landings - The Battle of Palermo**


Imitation, it is said, is the greatest form of flattery, and had Sultan Umar Abu Hafs Hafsid II been alive he would surely have been flattered by the Crusader’s choice of tactics next.  A split landing.

Some 11,000 Aragonese soldiers would make a mass landing at Palermo while some 16,000 Neapolitans would make a series of wave-landings at Syracuse in an attempt to surround the Tunisian army from both sides.

By this time however, Tunisian forces on the island had swelled to 20,000 Tunisians, after accounting for the casualties from Messina, 4,400 Sicilians, and 4,000 Mamluks.  In a bold move, the brothers al-Fadl and Ibrahim divided their army, each taking half to contest their respective landing.

al-Fadl took 10,400 men to Palermo, where the Sicilian forces were already waiting, and marched on the Aragonese forces attempting to take the city.  The battle was swift and decisive, seeing the Aragonese driven from the island with heavy losses.

The city was secured, and half of the battle was won, ending the threat to western Sicily.

events.author: JPThunda
events.snippet:
**The Second Landings - The Battle of Syracuse**


The second force, under Ibrahim, moved toward the ancient city of Syracuse.  Due to difficulties ne
events.fullText:
**The Second Landings - The Battle of Syracuse**


The second force, under Ibrahim, moved toward the ancient city of Syracuse.  Due to difficulties negotiating the terrain, they did not arrive until the city had already fallen, however the Neapolitans had only landed with 5,500 men due to the limitations of their ships.  By the time the Tunisian and Mamluk forces engaged, 11,000 Neapolitans had rallied on the island.

Ultimately it would prove insufficient, as the Neapolitan army was routed.  A third landing by the Neapolitans was attempted at Catania, however the 7,700 Tunisian survivors from Syracuse contested the landing, wiping out the invading force utterly.  After some time the countryside was secured, and the eastern threat had been likewise ended.

The late Sultan’s feat lay unrepeated, and the island remained secure.

events.author: JPThunda
events.snippet:
**Crusaders Repulsed & an end to Hostilities**


The failed invasions of Palermo and Syracuse heralded the end of the conflict.  The Crusaders, althou
events.fullText:
**Crusaders Repulsed & an end to Hostilities**


The failed invasions of Palermo and Syracuse heralded the end of the conflict.  The Crusaders, although having inflicted considerable damage to the Tunisian navy, were unable to enforce a blockade of Sicily.  As a result the Tunisian forces routed the Crusader armies utterly.  Sultan Abu Bakr Hafsid III offered extremely generous peace terms to the Crusaders–only a white peace–in the hopes that it would bring about a lasting end to the conflict.  Whether this is the case remains to be seen.

The Tunisian Sultanate owes tremendous gratitude to its allies.  To the Moroccan Sultanate for guaranteeing the security of North Africa, enabling the Sultan to devote the entirety of his forces to the Sicilian campaign.  To the Mamluk Sultanate of Egypt, for their honor and valor upon the field of battle, committing key forces to the conflict in defense of the Islamic world.  And, most critically of all, to the Roman Empire, without whom the war would have been surely lost.

The lasting impacts of the war are still being felt, but this has strengthened the somewhat tenuous bond between the Tunisian Sultanate and the Roman Empire.  A tyrant and a warmonger, the Catholic Pope in Rome has succeeded with threats, violence, and bounties in doing what centuries of peacemakers and diplomats failed to achieve–uniting Christians and Muslims together.  Unified by the common purpose of survival in the face of a belligerent and unrelenting foe who wages war against body and spirit, baptised in blood, forged and tempered in the conflagration of war, the **Roman-Tunisian Alliance** has now been quenched in triumph and victory.  What could make for a finer, more enduring mettle?

<@302128861209165824> <@874750350538727434> <@150411715757080576> <@327561459976372224> <@234699731715096576> <@1007414347347271730>

events.author: Cloud
events.snippet:
# The Dragon of France
Immense is his greed, great is the hoard he sits upon, and powerful is the crown that he wears. 

Once just known for his greed
events.fullText:
# The Dragon of France
Immense is his greed, great is the hoard he sits upon, and powerful is the crown that he wears. 

Once just known for his greed, now recognized as a dragon.

Not just for the strength of the crown he wears, the greed he is well known for, the pride he holds, the destructive actions done by him, nor the corruption he has conducted in private. He is the Dragon of France for he is hated by many, but rivaled by none. 

Standing as a beast who grows contempt basking in itself as others fail to be his equal.

## The League of the Griffin
-# <@255439449448644608> 
With the unpopularity of King Philippe VII de Valois illegal seizure, annexations, greed, pride, and destructiveness a new league vying for the French crown has risen. 

This is the League of the Griffin, and it is led by Duke Jehan II the Good de Valois. An older brother who is deeply unhappy with his younger brother’s embracement of his own sin, tyranny, and exhaustive greed. He desires to depose his younger brother, and end the rule of the Dragon. Ending the greed, destructiveness, and corruption found in his wake.

### Norman-Anjou Unification
The Duchy of Normandy and County of Anjou had grown close under Duke Jehan II the Good de Valois. He is likeable, honest, kind, calm, and all around a good man. Favorable traits that have allowed him to tighten the Norman union together over the years of his rule.

However, in dire times where France faces destruction at the hands of a Dragon, he must stand up against his sinful younger brother. Thus he calls for Parliament in Rouen for the entirety of the Norman Union. Here he swayed the estates of both partners of the Norman Union by appealing deeply to their emotions, their hatred for the King’s steep taxes, their sense of honor that King Phillippe VII tarnishes, and their hatred for the sins the King embraces. Swayed to raise even more levies against King Philippe VII despite the losses found against the HRE, swayed them to agree to unifying these two realms as one to hold the strength to contend with King Philippe VII might, and inspired them to fight for the rightful King of France. 

Only he can rule, not his younger brother who has proven himself unfit for rule or the Plantagenet who stand across the channel as pretenders holding onto an illegal claim.

### Rebellion
With the estates behind him, and his titles consolidated, he confidently sends forth a demand to his brother.

“To my villainous brother King Philippe VII de Valois, 
I request your immediate abdication, and that you surrender the crown of France to me. It is clearly evident that our father’s denial of allowing me to inherit the throne of France was made in great error, since you are most unfit to rule. Surrender now, and your punishment will be meager. Force me to fight you, and then only God can grant you mercy. Banished to a monastery where you will live the rest of your life to beg the Lord for forgiveness of your sins.

Sincerely, Duke Jehan II de Valois”

King Philippe had not reacted well to this betrayal by his own brother, and was greatly furious at such an obviously provoking message. HE is the KING of France, HE is the most wealthy and powerful of all of Christendom, HE is unchallengeable in the field of battle and rule! He will not entertain Duke Jehan II de Valois' insulting demand, and immediately declares him guilty of treason and declares war upon him.

||King Philippe VII will have his brother’s head, and he will annex his brother’s land. Slaying his kin, and his wife. Crushing any thinking that he could be challenged, and halting another claimant of the Kingdom of France.|| Although, is this truly an unexpected outcome if you were to look out at how King Philippe VII has treated his enemies in other wars?

Hearing of the declaration of war made by his brother, Duke Jehan would ready himself for war. To defeat the Dragon of France, and assert stability and prosperity that his brother denies.

### Seeking recognition and legitimacy
Claiming the French Kingdom is no easy task, nor is challenging King Philippe VII. Thus, Duke Jehan II offers up a proposed law to the vassals of France, informally referred to as the Law of the Griffin. In it he offers significantly reduced taxes for subjects of the French Crown, and informally suggests a promise to spend King Philippe VII great hoard for the recovery of France in face of King Phillippe VII greed and aggressiveness.

As it stands Bourbon, Angoulême, Etampes, Vendôme, Forez, Auvergne, Boulogne, Artois, Roucy, Comminges, and Mortain have pledged loyalty to Duke Jehan II bid for the crown of France. Possibly even more if other vassals are convinced as well <@1123821809704374313>  <@259426601865838592> . A development that has greatly disturbed King Philippe VII income from vassal payments, and shows the rise of the Griffin’s will to challenge the Dragon.

## The Dragon is tired

Brittany has been crushed, the Holy Roman Empire recently defeated, the Pope in Rome soon to be attacked and besieged by the mercenaries of France, the English soon to be engaged in battle in Brittany, and even Duke Jehan II now makes his bid for the crown of France. 

The Dragon is tired of conducting war, and has suffered two heart attacks already having to hire expensive mercenaries to fight the Papacy. So much gold and silver wasted on soldiers and war rather than being additions to his hoard. This displeases the Dragon, but his enemies are just far too envious of his riches to be ignored. He must continue to stand his ground, and continue to fend off his many enemies who fail to match his power. However, every single victory another crack is made on the skin of the Dragon. 

How much more could the Dragon of France bear before he collapses in defeat by his inferior enemies, or in battle against his own body?

### War with England never rests
Still, the Plantagenet claim to be the rightful rulers of France. Fortunately for them, King Philippe VII faces more foes at all fronts. Even within France itself with Duke Jehan II the Good and the Griffin rising in revolt. Unfortunately for them, now there is another that claims the same throne with growing popular support. 

Despite this, war with the English is never ending. King Philippe VII iron is tested, and another exhaustive battle is soon to take place in Brittany. One that would leave the armies of France even weaker when facing Duke Jehan II. However, if the English somehow evaded interception in Brittany, then maybe he could let Duke Jehan II and King Philippe VII wear each other out without fighting.

### At arms with an opposing Pope
“Pope” Phelix V Abate of Rome is nothing but a pretender the corrupt cardinals of the Holy Roman Empire, Italy and Hungary have recognized as their own for their hatred of King Philippe VII and his Kingdom. Obviously, this gives ||the even more corrupt and greedy|| King Philippe VII the noble and holy cause of utilizing the tithe gathered in France, Northern Italy, and Castile to develop support and recognition for the Pope to be based in Lyon. 

Surprisingly, the martial hound of Rome has taken the initiative against Lyon by seeking out war against the supporters of the Pope in Lyon starting with Ferrara. This has brought him Phelix V Abate and his forces to a precarious position. One where the wildly disorganized Hungarians sit separately from the Knights of Rhodes and the Papal levies. Ultimately giving way for the forces of Lyon to strike hard against the split forces of the more numerous armies of the supporters of Roma. However, the battle is still undecided and the rivers of Ferrara betray its people by giving the supporters of Roma an advantage to work with alongside a numerical advantage.

### Calling upon Auld Alliance
-# <@1417260821674852363> 
The Auld Alliance had remained unutilized for decades, yet this had not meant it would remain so forever. King Philippe VII, recognizing the threat his brother presents, needs less pressure from the King of England. 

Thus, he would play his hand, and attempt to sway the Lowlanders to conduct raids upon England. Doing so by sending forth a diplomat to the court of Alba ||with an unknown and limited budget on what he's allowed to offer. The Dragon has tightened its grip on its treasury, but coins still come loose as the Kingdom of France faces more and more foes||. King Philippe VII heavily hopes that Scottish raids would give him the time he needs to push back against his ever growing number of enemies.
