events.author: Hermans
events.snippet:
**Saint’s Cathedral**

Soon after Ladislau’s sainthood, Sânladislau began constructing a cathedral in his honor in the town's main square. For the cat
events.fullText:
**Saint’s Cathedral**

Soon after Ladislau’s sainthood, Sânladislau began constructing a cathedral in his honor in the town's main square. For the cathedral, Sânladislau sought the best architect that could be found on the Balkan Peninsula. The construction of the cathedral began slowly, but brick by brick the image of the final structure gradually started to appear, and with it the pace of construction quickened. Upon its completion, the cathedral became the centerpiece of Sânladislau.

The Orthodox opposition was outraged by the cathedral’s completion, as it marked the point at which the town had been ceded to Catholic influence and the Orthodox lost all control over the religious affairs of Sânladislau (or Zimnicea, as the Orthodox priests still called it). They gazed upon the cathedral with disdain and malicious intent.

events.author: ST Peter
events.snippet:
**The Church bells across Europe ring! The pious weep with unimaginable bliss! And the whole of Christendom rejoices together!**
# For the Kingdom of
events.fullText:
**The Church bells across Europe ring! The pious weep with unimaginable bliss! And the whole of Christendom rejoices together!**
# For the Kingdom of Jerusalem, which rules over the sacred land of the Bible, has been restored!

**An opportunity arises: 1404 - 1409**
How quickly the tides of fortune shift. In 1404 King Peter I of Cyprus, Jerusalem, and Cilicia held out little hope of reclaiming the lands that his Kingships entitled him to. The Roman Catholic world had only just united itself thanks to the steel swords of the French Crown <@460814348487753730> . Peter I had been drinking himself into stupors every night by then, but still there remained some small hope that support could be gathered for a crusade, even if its success was not assured. Across the Levantine Sea however, were scenes of mounting chaos. The Mamluks <@874750350538727434> , after centuries of rule, had begun to fray as internal instability took hold in the Sultanate. For years the chaos in Egypt and the Levant ripped away the Mamluks ability to properly defend its long frontiers, and Cypriot raiders soon found the Mamluk coasts easy prey.

Stung into action by a perhaps sole opportunity to have an advantage against the once powerful Mamluk armies, King Peter I de Lusignan redoubled his efforts in trying to organize a crusade in the east. Christendom as a whole was uncertain of the prospects, but King Henry II of France proved much more receptive to the idea. Eventually, after much deliberation he would take the cross, and pledge the resources of the French Crown to the crusade. Perhaps, to a cynical mind, he did not do this out of a noble piety, but more pragmatically sought to unite the Church's many divides by accomplishing this great task, which would doubtlessly grant him and the Lyon Papacy immense prestige and legitimacy were it to succeed.

Soon afterwards, the Kings of Castile <@422498641425596418>  and Aragon <@1007414347347271730> respectively took up the cross as well. It is not surprising why King Alfonso XII of Castile did so, for his future royal line would probably inherit all of the Lusignan Kingdoms thanks to the union between Prince Fadrique and Princess Catherine, but King Ferran I of Aragon's intervention was not expected. His joining might be the one most likely born of pure piety and not necessarily pragmatism. Though, it is likely that the loss of Sardinia to the Sultanate of Carthage inspired vengeance against Muslims, even if they were not Carthaginian. After many months of planning and organizing, the crusade was ready to begin in early 1409, and the reclamation of the Holy Land was an vow that all swore to accomplish.

**The Tenth Crusade: 1409 - 1410**

King Peter I was elated simply to have a crusade begun, much less one that involved three powerful Kings. Unlike the later part of the 14th century, the Lusignan King had hope again, and the determination not to fail at this golden opportunity that God had granted him. He had spent the last months in Europe, meeting with several rulers and dredging up willing mercenary bands who might join the crusade in exchange for coin. With this enthusiastic force, paid well by Peter I, he set off for Antioch in early 1409, after the crusade had officially begun. To the surprise of the Lusignan King and his forces, the state of the defenses in the Levant was even worse than they could have dreamed of. Garrisons were abandoning their posts to secure the safety of their own families, armed resistance was little, and the feared Mamluk armies were trapped in Egypt fighting a massive civil war as chaos continued to mount in the crumbling Sultanate.

Peter I's army easily stormed the powerful, yet scarcely defended, walls of Antioch, and Aleppo was sacked for its plentiful supplies.

The Syrian army, still nominally under the authority of the Mamluk Sultan but in reality completely independent by this point, tried to cut off the crusader forces by attempting to retake Antioch, but they were routed in battle by Peter I. Many thousands were killed and hundreds captured, which were ransomed back for a hefty sum. 

With the Syrian force annihilated, the road down the Levantine coast was opened, and Peter I, along with a contingent of the French army, advanced south. Back up north in Cilicia and Antioch, the armies of Castile and Aragon gathered, but it was quickly becoming apparent that they would not be needed, as the Levant was in even more chaos than was anticipated. Outside Acre stood the last resistance that the Muslims could muster to face the crusade, the army of Filastin, which was roughly the same size of the Syrian force who fought at Antioch. Like the Syrians before them however, this force didn't stand a chance against the motivated and skilled crusaders. The Muslim force guarding Acre was destroyed, but in contrast to the rest of the Mamluk Levant, the garrison at Acre did not capitulate. Instead, they chose to hold out for as long as they could against the Christian invaders. With the still resisting towns and forts and cities back up north being mopped up by the Duke Phillip II of Savoy's <@564196329132261376>  crusading force, Peter I prepared to strike into the heart of the Mamluk realm itself: Egypt.

Yet, Sultan Isa I realized the hopelessness of the situation. Any forces he sent to defend against the crusader forces would only draw precious manpower away from fighting the increasingly bloody civil war in Egypt, and so in 1410 he offered terms to Peter I. It was a joyous moment for the aging King. Now the master of the Levant...he surprisingly enforced lenient terms on the Sultan. Antioch and Palestine would be his to rule, of course, but he did not take the whole of the Levantine coast.

King Peter I de Lusignan was always a cautious man, born from the chaos of his early reign. Now, at the moment of his greatest triumph, he was careful again. This time he was probably being careful not to invoke the wrath of the whole of the Islamic world by taking too much. Instead, he took what he deemed was too important to let slip and returned the rest to the Syrians.

With the crusade over then, it was time for solemn piety. After the terms were fulfilled and the Mamluk garrisons in Palestine withdrawn, King Peter I de Lusignan traveled to the Holy City of Jerusalem, and went into the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, where he gave his grateful thanks to God. The Tenth Crusade was done, and it had succeeded in its task, which only the First Crusade three centuries ago could claim to have accomplished in equal measure. With the proclamation of the Kingdom's restoration, the church bells across Europe rung, the pious wept with bliss, and the whole of Christendom could rejoice together. For the first time in two centuries Jerusalem was in the hands of Catholics oncemore.

Now it was time to rebuild the Kingdom from the ashes, and secure it so that it may never fall again...

-# The Tenth Crusade

**(End)**

events.author: JPThunda
events.snippet:
**The Sardinian Conflict**


The conflict in Sardinia, as it came to be called in the Carthaginian court, was a relatively minor affair in terms of ov
events.fullText:
**The Sardinian Conflict**


The conflict in Sardinia, as it came to be called in the Carthaginian court, was a relatively minor affair in terms of overall combat, though the political ramifications, while not immediately manifested, ultimately outweighed any military losses.

The Aragonese, despite being outclassed on the waves, refused to surrender the island to the Roman Claim of Eirini Palaiologos, and so the conflict began in late 1404.  A landing of forces on Sardinia was unopposed, and indeed the Aragonese forces and fleet would make no showing for nearly the entire two years of the conflict.  The same could not be said of their Neapolitan ally and their client states.  Although ineffectual, they were meddlesome.

The Albanian army attempted an invasion of Algiers and was swiftly repulsed.  During the middle of the second year of the conflict, while the greater part of the Carthaginian fleet was busy hunting the Sardinian ships and scouting for the Aragonese, a small force of Neapolitans were landed upon Sicily at Palermo.  Despite their bravery, their position was hopeless, and terms were offered to the Aragonese to surrender Sardinia and end the conflict without further bloodshed.

The Aragonese delayed their negotiations, using the Neapolitan forces as bait to lure out the Carthaginian transports, and attempted a brazen raid against the embarked Carthaginian forces.  The raid was poorly carried out, however, and the ships–and their embarked soldiers–handily evaded the Aragonese, delivering their forces to Palermo where they soundly defeated the Neapolitan forces in detail.

Only once the Carthaginian fleet began their pursuit of the Aragonese navy did the Aragonese crown show a sense of urgency in their negotiations, quickly surrendering the island of Sardinia before the Aragonese fleet could be brought to battle and destroyed.  Shortly after the war, the Sultan named his nephew as Governor of Sardinia on his behalf, severing the union of the ousted Barcelona royals.

<@460814348487753730> <@422498641425596418> <@234699731715096576> <@1007414347347271730>

events.author: JPThunda
events.snippet:
**Sardinia - Aftermath**



The subjugation of Sardinia, bringing it more fully into the Carthaginian market, sparked nothing less than an economic mi
events.fullText:
**Sardinia - Aftermath**



The subjugation of Sardinia, bringing it more fully into the Carthaginian market, sparked nothing less than an economic miracle on the island.  The abundance of cheap building supplies now available enabled the Sardinians to embark on major building programs, further building the Sardinian economy.  The economy came to rapidly rival the Sicilian economy which had utterly eclipsed it mere years prior.  Some signs of conflict remain fresh, however for the vast majority of Sardinians, Carthaginian rule has been a marked improvement.

Beyond the island of Sardinia, however, the Catholic world took notice.  Another major Mediterranean island had been reclaimed by the North African Sultanate, and more Catholics brought under their rule.  The Sultan was a tolerant man and it showed–it could be said that Catholics lived more privileged lives than his Muslim subjects with markedly lower rates of taxation, and their communities thrived, but this only added more fuel to the fires of Catholic intolerance and envy.  Despite support by the Sicilian Catholics and the Sultan in the Lyonnese conflict with Rome, Sardinia became the justification for the Catholics to engage in acts of piracy, although the truth and extents of this would take nearly a decade to come to light…

events.author: Hoang
events.snippet:
# The Promises of the Visconti

In contrast to the Italianzug, Emperor Giovonni Maria embarked on a years long Germanzug, touring the lands of Germany
events.fullText:
# The Promises of the Visconti

In contrast to the Italianzug, Emperor Giovonni Maria embarked on a years long Germanzug, touring the lands of Germany to gather support for the passing of Imperial taxation

To secure the passage of Imperial taxation and strengthen his position within the Empire, Emperor Giovanni Maria Visconti made promises to many princes, bishops and cities. Some were clear grants of land or gold. Others were vague, leaving room for interpretation.

- Ferrara shall receive three provinces from Mantua. <@315868897012219904> 

- Trier shall receive gold, along with Imperial support for bringing the town of Besacon under their rule. <@137101075411894272> 

- The Habsburgs were to be granted access to the sea through Trieste. <@190640828954181632> 

- Lusatia League shall receive formal recognition as a state. In addition, the Lusatia League is granted the title of Margraves and are tasked to defend the Empire's northern frontier.  <@255439449448644608> 

- The Hanseatic Order shall receive money as support for the improvement of the northern towns. <@288410456526422016> 

- Berg shall receive the submission of the city of Dortmund. However, the wording of the promise remains ambiguous: it is unclear whether Dortmund is merely to submit to Berg’s authority, or whether the city is to be fully annexed. <@792904515795550218>

- Ruhr shall receive support for the consolidation of Munster, though the Emperor’s definition of “Munster” remains unclear. Whether this refers only to the city itself, its surrounding lands, or a broader territorial claim has been left vauge. <@253679178552049665> 

- Brabant shall receive an Imperial edict legitimizing the unification of the Netherlands should they choose to do so. <@99704182809853952> 

- Bavaria shall receive Imperial support for the unification of Wittelsbach lands under a single ruler, as well as changes to succession law. Due to the vague wording, it is unclear whether the Emperor meant only the current Bavarian lands, or ALL lands held by the Wittelsbach dynasty. <@763652399784919080> 

- Zurich shall receive bribes worth up to 160 gold. <@883471267506843668> 

Thus were the promises of the Visconti Emperor: some written plainly, others vauge. This may soon become the source of new disputes, as every prince now reads the Emperor’s words in the way most favorable to themself.

The Emperor himself will also unlikely be able to resolve disputes between Princes because for the German part of the Empire, **the Alps are high and the Emperor is far away**.

Whoever is the next Emperor will have their work cut out for them.

events.author: DerNette
events.snippet:
# The Twilight of House d'Alsace

The flame of House d'Alsace burns ever dimmer.

Of the once numerous children of the dynasty, only a handful remain.
events.fullText:
# The Twilight of House d'Alsace

The flame of House d'Alsace burns ever dimmer.

Of the once numerous children of the dynasty, only a handful remain. Sexy d'Alsace serves as Bishop of Münster, Tom d'Alsace as Bishop of Strasbourg, and Tinti d'Alsace as Archbishop of Trier. Catarina d'Alsace rules as Countess of Rochechouart, while the head of the family resides in Lorraine as Duke.

The story of the house began with Raoul d'Alsace, the man who restored unity and stability to the Moselle region through the conquest of Metz. In doing so, he not only strengthened the bonds between Lorraine and the Bishopric of Trier, but also laid the foundations upon which the prosperity of the duchy would later be built.
Yet Raoul's age ended in war. Standing alongside his fellow Imperial Princes, he found himself embroiled in a conflict against France. The war left scars not only upon the land, but also upon the heart of his son and heir.

Tombo d'Alsace inherited a duchy devastated by conflict. The fields had to be restored, the towns rebuilt, and confidence in the future rekindled. It was under his rule that Lorraine demonstrated that the House of d'Alsace was capable of far more than warfare. Economic growth, administration, and long-term planning became the hallmarks of his reign.
Even before inheriting, Tombo had distinguished himself in government. He served within the ducal cabinet from a young age, earning a reputation for diligence and competence. While his father fought wars abroad, Tombo quietly learned the art of governing. Though he lost his father early, he was never alone. His many siblings remained close allies, none more important than his brother Tinti d'Alsace, whose position in Trier would become invaluable to Lorraine's future.

Like his father before him, Tombo would eventually lead armies into battle. This time, however, the conflict was against the Emperor himself. Believing that Imperial authority had been abused against Lorraine's close allies in House Wittelsbach, Tombo joined the struggle personally. Marching at the head of his armies as a general, he sought to honor the memory of his father.
The war became Lorraine's coming of age. For the first time, the duchy demonstrated its strength beyond its immediate cultural sphere. With some of the highest participation in the conflict, Lorraine emerged with greatly increased prestige and influence throughout the Empire.
Late in life, at the age of forty-three, Tombo finally secured the continuation of his line through the birth of a son. His daughter, originally expected to inherit, remained in France as Countess of Rochechouart, where she too established a family through marriage to a Wittelsbach.
The final years of Tombo's reign were marked not by war, but by reconciliation. Through the efforts of his brother Tinti, important contacts were established with the French Papacy. Old wounds between Lorraine and France slowly began to heal. Tombo hoped that his son would inherit a realm of peace rather than one burdened by devastation and reconstruction.
When Tombo II eventually succeeded his father after his peaceful passing, he inherited a duchy richer and stronger than ever before. Roads, markets, and towns flourished. Infrastructure stretched across the land, and prosperity flowed through the Moselle valley.

The young duke sought to build upon this success. Metz, already the economic heart of Lorraine, was gradually transformed into a spiritual center following the recognition of the French Papacy. At the same time, Nancy was elevated into a great city intended to serve as the secular capital of the duchy.

Under his direction new towns were founded and expanded at Darney, Arches, Morhange, and along the Saar. The duchy entered an age of modernization and growth unmatched in its history.
Yet fate proved cruel.
A devastating outbreak of smallpox swept through the land and claimed all of the duke's children. The tragedy shattered him. Though the duchy remained prosperous, Tombo II never fully recovered from the loss.
And so the question now hangs over Lorraine.


Who shall inherit the legacy of House d'Alsace?
Two legitimate claimants stand before the realm.
The first is Adol Wittelsbach, eldest son of Catarina d'Alsace, Countess of Rochechouart. His succession would draw Lorraine more firmly into Imperial politics and greatly strengthen the influence of House Wittelsbach. Yet it would likely halt the long process of rapprochement with France that had defined the final years of Tombo's reign.
The second is Edouard de Dampierre, son of Isabelle d'Alsace and heir to the County of Namur. Among the nobility of Lorraine he is by far the more popular candidate. Raised in Lorraine from childhood and appointed to positions within the ducal administration at a young age, he is well known to both nobles and commoners alike. He understands the vision of House d'Alsace and is committed to carrying it forward. Furthermore, he enjoys the support of Trier and many of Lorraine's most influential families.
Yet his succession would not come without danger. The Wittelsbachs possess a legitimate claim of their own, and few doubt that they would contest any inheritance that passed them by.
Thus Tombo II faces the greatest challenge of his life.
Not the building of cities. Not the waging of wars. Not the expansion of markets or the strengthening of alliances.
Instead, he must decide who shall inherit Lorraine when the final chapter of House d'Alsace comes to an end.
For he knows that he is likely living through the final act of a dynasty that has shaped the Moselle for generations.

events.author: King Jester The III
events.snippet:
**The Stablization of the Electorate of Brandenburg and the Limbourg Miracle**
<@255439449448644608> <@545657694489542666> <@112960565344804864> 
From
events.fullText:
**The Stablization of the Electorate of Brandenburg and the Limbourg Miracle**
<@255439449448644608> <@545657694489542666> <@112960565344804864> 
From the fall of the Duchy of Brandenburg, Immanuel Von Limbourg, in his final days, had ordered that Altmark must hold, and it did, thanks to the efforts of drilling the militia, which could now stand on its own two legs. However, it came at the cost of only Altmark, and her sister province stayed with the "Wittelsbach" on paper, but has now been transferred to the Limburgs and the Duchy of Berg to help bring it back. Additionally, the nobles of Berg, Ruhr, and now Altmark had begun to form a bloc in the diet to force through a law strengthening the Nobility even more to keep Raphael in check once Immanuel passed away. With Adolph the X Von Limbourg being too young, the nobility had most sway not over the county or duchy but over decisions. The first matter was to get Dortmund, as the nobles of Ruhr had long looked with envy and struck at the permission Berg had to take the free city under the banner of Berg; on paper, it was, in fact, under Ruhr to unite the fractured Westphalia. The second matter was to make sure the portion of Limbourg was taken back with negotiations between Cologne, allowing them to take it as nobles in the border region between the two, where the nobles in the border region between the two were dissatisfied and wanted to join the growing movement within Cologne to gain more influence over the majority of lower Franconians and the Ripuarian Franconians, being the majority, and accepted than the natural-born Moselle.

events.author: King Jester The III
events.snippet: The third matter was under Luciano Visconti, as he was the one who convinced Immanuel during his twilight to side with Milan rather than Bavaria. This
events.fullText:
The third matter was under Luciano Visconti, as he was the one who convinced Immanuel during his twilight to side with Milan rather than Bavaria. This decision of Luciano landed Berg a position, the amount of leverage it gave him, not just as a mere general but as a close advisor to the new duke, and the surge in Renaissance interest as the university spread through the Berg-Ruhr and the introduction of a new man of arms and drilling to keep up militarily under Richard Von Gladbach a officer who was under Wilhelm for Brandenburg and Dortmund.

events.author: ST Peter
events.snippet:
# Restoring the Crusader Kingdom.
**Rewards for the Crusaders: 1410**

From Sidon to Ascalon and beyond the newly restored Kingdom of Jerusalem now ru
events.fullText:
# Restoring the Crusader Kingdom.
**Rewards for the Crusaders: 1410**

From Sidon to Ascalon and beyond the newly restored Kingdom of Jerusalem now ruled. It controlled the many Holy cities of early Christianity, like Bethlehem, Nazareth, and Hebron. The last holdouts of the Kingdom before it fell in 1291 were also restored, like Acre and Tyre. Garrisons were put in each place, and the task now fell to another matter. With the Tenth Crusade having reached its successful conclusion, now was the time for the victorious crusaders to receive their rewards. This was done to give thanks of course, but also to perhaps convince some of the knights and soldiers to stay in the Kingdom. Good Christians, nobles in particular, were much sought after by King Peter I. Titles, gifts, and other honors were subsequently bestowed on those who had helped liberate the Holy Land, including the rulers of Europe, with the Duke of Savoy <@564196329132261376> being granted the title "Prince of Galilee", and the King of France <@460814348487753730>  being proclaimed the "Protector of Outremer", with the Kings of Castile <@422498641425596418>  and Aragon <@1007414347347271730>  both being made lieutenants of the Kingdom of Jerusalem. Peter I knew how much he'd need reliable allies both locally and in Christendom itself if his restored Kingdom was to last. He could not make the same mistakes of his father King Guy II, who lost Cilicia shortly after gaining it due to a lack of friendly allies to call upon in times of need.

events.author: ST Peter
events.snippet: So with that, many of the crusaders would return to their homes in Europe. Like in the First Crusade, it was likely that these returning crusaders wou
events.fullText:
So with that, many of the crusaders would return to their homes in Europe. Like in the First Crusade, it was likely that these returning crusaders would become local celebrities for what they had accomplished in the east. The Pope Innocent V would also in all likelihood receive a major boost to his prestige and legitimacy, for few Popes before him have ever organized such a successful crusading venture. He certainly had the profound thanks of King Peter I and the restored Latin Patriarch of Jerusalem.


**The Return to Cilicia and the death of King Peter I de Lusignan: 1411 - 1412**

Soon after the reconquest of the Holy Land, Peter I's sights turned north, to the other Kingdom he still claimed, that of Cilicia. Still ruled by the Turkish Beylik of the Dulkadirids, the region of Cilicia had been a chaotic one ever since 1370, when the Mamluks conquered it. Armenian revolts against the tyrannical Beylik had been a common occurrence, and the Bey didn't seem able to solve the problem by the time of the Mamluk civil war. With the Mamluks withdrawing from the region, the Dulkadirids were an easy prey that King Peter I prepared to kill before the great majority of his crusader forces returned to Europe. Unfortunately for the old Monarch, his years of indulgent drinking had finally caught up with him. On July 9th, 1411, whilst still preparing for the imminent invasion he collapsed suddenly and died. There was nothing the royal physicians could do for him, and preparations were solemnly made for his funeral. Like the Kings of Jerusalem before him, King Peter I was buried in the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, which had been reconsecrated shortly after the end of the crusade.

With King Peter I's death, it was finally time for his only child, Princess Catherine de Lusignan, to be crowned. First she was acclaimed, crowned, and anointed Queen of Jerusalem in the Church of the Holy Sepulchre by the Latin Patriarch Francisco Clemente Pérez Capera. Shortly afterwards, she returned to her old home of Cyprus to receive the Cypriot crown in Nicosie. Lastly, she readied herself to be crowned Queen of Cilicia, but under pressure of the belligerent nobility she proclaimed she would only be crowned such in Sis, the old capital of Cilicia. Sis was still under control of the Turks, so the message here was clear. Just months later, the army of Jerusalem advanced into the Cilician heartland almost unopposed. The Armenian nobility aided the Lusignan's return, for they welcomed a restoration of their power under the decentralized rule of the Franks. The Beylik could offer up little resistance in response, for their realm had only survived due to Mamluk aid, which was now firmly ended. Within a few months the whole of Cilicia was restored, and true to her promise Catherine was acclaimed Queen of Cilicia in Sis itself in early 1412. At last, all three of the Lusignan Kingdoms were *true* Kingdoms, owned not just in name but also in land and power.

Importantly, the army of Jerusalem did not move beyond the eastern mountains of Cilicia, which is where the true heartland of the Dulkadirids lay. The Frankish nobility considered it not worth the effort and manpower to march into the mountains to completely destroy their hated enemies, and instead swayed the Queen to end the war with what was gained. To the delight of the Armenians, the Beylik would indeed soon be destroyed, but not by the Queen's forces...

-# (Left to Right) The Death of King Peter I, the Storming of Tarsus.

events.author: ST Peter
events.snippet:
**Restoration of the Administration: 1413 - 1427**

Peace at last reigned over the Kingdoms. Yet now the Kingdoms had to learn to function oncemore. I
events.fullText:
**Restoration of the Administration: 1413 - 1427**

Peace at last reigned over the Kingdoms. Yet now the Kingdoms had to learn to function oncemore. It had been over a century since the Christians owned any land in the levant, and it had been *well* over a century since they owned so much as they did now. The task of restoring the administration to be anywhere near efficient in the newly reconquered lands would be a difficult one. Firstly of course, the exiled crusader nobility and clergy who had spent the last century on Cyprus returned to the Outremer to reclaim their old titles, and make them no longer titular. The Latin Patriarch of Jerusalem returned to the city, ousting the Orthodox Patriarch from the Holy Sepulchre. The Ibelins returned to rule the towns of Ibelin and Ramla, the Lords of Tyre, Caesarea, and Arsuf all returned as well. Queen Catherine I was powerless to stop this rapid decentralization of the Kingdom of Jerusalem, though it was doubtful she wanted to stop it at all. The Crown itself was already struggling to control the unruly population of the towns it directly controlled, thus the nobility taking effective control over much of the Holy Lands alleviated some of the overburdened administration of the royal domains.

The naturally unruly Muslim and Orthodox populations, none too pleased to be under the control of Catholics oncemore, would revolt several times over the following decade, though these were all stamped down by the Kingdom's army. This persisted until Queen Catherine I finally relented in late 1425, granting the non-Catholic population guarantees and concessions that they would not face persecution or mass forced conversions, like in the old Kingdom.

A certain harmony has now emerged in the past couple short years, a return to the strangely peaceful rule of the first Kingdom of Jerusalem. The Catholics were still doubtlessly the elite of the country, but below that class there was a genuine mixture of cultures and traditions and systems of administrating the populace. The Iqta, for instance, would remain undisturbed by the returning crusaders. The matters of the Muslims and Eastern Christians would, after the fervor of the victorious crusade faded, would begin to be simply ignored by the Catholics, as the feudal system took hold oncemore in the Holy Land.

-# The Royal Capital of Jerusalem

events.author: ST Peter
events.snippet:
**Acre, the old Economic Hub: 1427**

Acre was a vital city to the old Kingdom of Jerusalem. It was through here that trade flowed from the east to th
events.fullText:
**Acre, the old Economic Hub: 1427**

Acre was a vital city to the old Kingdom of Jerusalem. It was through here that trade flowed from the east to the west. Thanks to that, the city was a bustling center filled with all manners of people, whether they be Italians, Arabs, or Franks, all seeking to make their fortunes trading the valuable goods to the ravenous ports of Europe. Now, with the return of the Crusader Kingdom, Acre has oncemore seen an economic boom. With the adminstration of the Kingdom only slowly being restored, taxes have been inefficient and thus trade has been vital to fill the royal treasury. Great efforts have been made to restore Acre to the true market center it once was, and with the amount of goods flowing through it, that is doubtful to take much time. Furthermore, it is likely that the Italian Merchant Republics are to receive many privileges in the near future to incentivize more trade through the ancient port city.

(I will be creating a market center in Acre in the near future).

events.author: ST Peter
events.snippet:
**May the Kingdom never Fall Again.**

Securing the internal stability of the Kingdom was one thing, but it was another to protect it from outside thr
events.fullText:
**May the Kingdom never Fall Again.**

Securing the internal stability of the Kingdom was one thing, but it was another to protect it from outside threats, most prominently a resurgent Mamluk Sultanate. Fortunately for the Queen of Jerusalem, friends were abound. Her marriage to Prince Fadrique de Trastámara all but guaranteed Castilian aid, and much effort was made by the Queen to keep the French Crown invested in the fate of the Holy Land. Diplomats from Jerusalem flooded the French court, all aiming to keep the King loyally committed to the cause of the Cross. When in 1420 the King of Castile died, that made Queen Catherine's husband, Fadrique, the new King of Castile, Leon, and Portugal. Naturally then he had to leave for Iberia, but the Queen stayed behind to rule her own Kingdoms. This was a sad experience, but still extremely beneficial for the Crusader Kingdom. With the ascension of King Fadrique to the Castilian throne, aid from Iberia was not just *almost* certain, but instead *completely* assured. 

Yet, aid from Europe should not be the only foreign protection sought. It would take months for aid to arrive from so far away in any conflict, and so the Frankish nobility sought out a closer ally with which to seek an accommodation with. Among these options, two seemed most likely. The Empire of Constantinople <@302128861209165824>  and the Karamanid Sultanate <@1216097200401154181> . Contrary to what one might imagine in a simple black and white world, the Karamanids and the Lusignans had been on good terms since the late 1360's, despite their different faith and previous conflicts. When King Guy II de Lusignan first conquered Cilicia, he stopped his occasional raids on the southern Anatolian coast and opened negotiations with Fahreddin Ahmed Bey. A deal was reached in which the Lusignans would pay the Karamanids to rein in their unruly Turkish raiders from attacking western Cilicia.

The deal was beneficial to both sides, and proved that limited cooperation was possible between the two sides. Unfortunately for Guy II, Cilicia was soon lost to the Mamluks, and the deal ended, but the good relations did not. During the reign of King Peter I, the raids on the southern Anatolian coast never resumed, and eventually a deal was struck where he purchased the fort of Silifke in return for a large one-time tribute and the renunciation of the rest of the Cilician claims that the Karamanids owned. With yet another peaceful deal reached between the Turks and Franks, it seemed as if there was a growing understanding between the two realms.

Until, at last, the Tenth Crusade secured the Holy Land oncemore for the Lusignans, and the reconquest of Cilicia occurred not long after. To the delight of the Kingdom, the Karamanids quickly finished off the hated Dulkadirid Beylik, and a short peace ensued over Anatolia. That was until a war broke out between the Empire of Constantinople and the Karamanids. In return for the kind relations with the Karamanids, and the Sultan's neutrality during the crusade, the Kingdom never seriously considered intervening against the Turks during this war over the fate of Anatolia. The Karamanids, as has been seen, had proven to be good neighbors, if not friends, to the eastern Kingdoms, even if the conquest of the Anatolian lands owned by the Knights Hospitaller did irk many zealous knights and nobles. 

With the Karamanids eventually emerging triumphant after the bloody years long conflict, there was not a subtle amount of satisfaction at the result from many of the old nobles in the Kingdom, some still having remembered when the Byzantines ignored their pleas for help when the Mamluks invaded Cilicia in the late 1360's.

Now was the time to restore ties to the victor. In the 1420's, talks began between the Karamanid Sultanate and the Kingdom of Jerusalem, with Queen Catherine I initially offering a restoration of the treaty that her grandfather had with the Karamanid Bey back in the 1360's. Yet, what was clear was that the Karamanids wanted more from the arrangement, and despite some pressures from her Iberian husband to reject a defensive alliance, the Queen was eager for the same thing. For, in her judgement, the treaty would seem to grant to both states the protection they sought. Jerusalem would have local aid if the Mamluks returned to the Levant, and Konya would have the aid of the Frankish knights if the vengeful Byzantines tried to reestablish dominance over Anatolia.

**So, in December 1426, the Treaty of Silifke was agreed upon!**

In its terms are many matters, including the reaffirmation of both sides that they desire no territory from the other, amongst small things like where exactly in the mountains do the borders lay.

Most importantly however, is the establishment of a defensive pact between the Kingdoms of Jerusalem, Cyprus, and Cilicia, and Karamanid Sultanate.

It is clear in how it is written that this is not an outright alliance, where they will join each other in offensive wars, but it is a guarantee for both states to protect the other if they called for military aid. Queen Catherine I's husband, the King of Castile, has also agreed to participate in the pact, but to a much lesser degree than the two principle parties. Instead, the Iberians role would probably be mostly economic if the Karamanids came under attack, which is still very beneficial. With any luck, this treaty will ensure the stability of Anatolia and the Levant. Even if it doesn't, at least each side will have an ally in the wars to come...

-# Frankish diplomats meeting with Sultan Bulend I Karamanoğlu

events.author: ST Peter
events.snippet:
---

**An Old Friend.**

So, with the Kingdom being secured internally and externally, it is hoped that efforts can truly begin to attract settlers fr
events.fullText:
---

**An Old Friend.**

So, with the Kingdom being secured internally and externally, it is hoped that efforts can truly begin to attract settlers from the west to settle in the Holy Land. Incentives are offered to any nobles and knights and lesser soldiers who would brave the journey to the east, to defend the most devout of Kingdoms from its enemies. To potential nobility at least the Kingdom must seem at least somewhat of an attractive prospect. The Queen of Jerusalem regularly seeks the expertise of the nobility in ruling her domain, and thanks to the many concessions to the nobility in the previous centuries, it is a powerful force indeed. To join its ranks would doubtlessly mean great power and wealth. For soldiers and and knights the prospects are not quite as lucrative, and their settling in the Holy Land would probably have to do more with personal piety than the desire for riches. Even still, the Queen would pay well for their service and their settling in the Holy Land, no matter how lowly a soldier they were.

In particular, the Queen wishes to reintegrate the Knights Hospitaller into the Kingdom. This Holy Order is one of the most prominent remaining in the Catholic east after the Templars were destroyed and the Teutons went to crusade in Prussia. The Knights Hospitaller has seen better days in truth. Its Italian lands were lost when the Roman Pope fell, and its Anatolian holdings were seized by the Karamanids not long after. Now confined to Rhodes oncemore, Queen Catherine I would offer them not to become Italian puppets or an independent order without much purpose, but to have a true place in the Kingdom of Jerusalem oncemore. She would send diplomats to the Knights headquarters on Rhodes, and over the following years they would negociate a deal. By 1427, this is what the deal offered the Knights:

**If the Knights Hospitaller would become subservient oncemore to the Queen of Jerusalem and her descendants, they would be granted the Duchy of Antioch to guard against the Kingdom's enemies. Furthermore, the Queen and her descendants will swear an oath to continue to maintain the Knights Order financially, to pay for its many upkeeps and ensure the Order will continue to thrive in the Holy Land.**

With the offer made, it was yet to see if the Grandmaster of the Order would accept, and return the duties of his Knights to protecting the Holy Land and ensuring the safety of pilgrimages to the Holy Cities...

-# The Wandering Knight, guarding the route of pilgrimage to Jerusalem

**(End)**

### Dyn Nod - A Man’s Mark

The old Uchelwr (High Men) were gone, that much was certain. The up
events.fullText:
### Dyn Nod - A Man’s Mark
-# ||(Pun on the work ‘marked’ - can mean both ways)||

The old Uchelwr (High Men) were gone, that much was certain. The uprisings of the past decades had wiped away the old orders of local Breyrs (councilors) in favor of English appointed judges and barons. Though Wales has been under the High Lordship of English sovereigns for some 300+ years it had never yet seen the systemic replacement of its local leadership like after the [Penal Laws of 1404](https://discord.com/channels/507915886477312023/1505545331591610549/1516269508027744406). In absence of the former local nobility and representation what was left?

**A void**. One increasingly filled by Welsh Bwrdeisiaid (Burghers), Dynion Newydd (New Men), and clever Swyddogion (Officials) with strong enough ties to the English administration to overlook their place of birth.

These men are made to leave their marks on documents as an officially recorded part of their work. Signatures can be forged and orders fabricated in this, but marks are known and nearly impossible to replicate. Thus it is customary for any man of means, a gentleman - Pobl Dda, in Wales to wear a signet ring of some descript. Not always fancy, rarely consistent, but always personalized. It is this growing class of English speaking people that now make up a growing proportion of walled settlements like Cardiff, Pembroke, and Carnarvon.

*These, and a vast array of other precious metal objects, are produced by the jewelry workshops of Pembroke. They take the silver of Aberystwyth, the rare [Welsh gold](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Welsh_gold) of Roman Dolaucothi, and the baser metals of the north and turn them into works of art. These are then provided to men of means across the isles and beyond - a spot of decided prosperity for the relatively cosmopolitan towns of Southern Wales.*

-# A decorative fede ring made for a well-to-do family in Wrexham. It bears the inscription: "de bon cuer - of good heart"

events.author: JPThunda
events.snippet:
**The Integration of Sicily into Carthage**


Since the 1340’s the island of Sicily stood a degree apart from the Carthaginian Sultanate, however afte
events.fullText:
**The Integration of Sicily into Carthage**


Since the 1340’s the island of Sicily stood a degree apart from the Carthaginian Sultanate, however after nearly a century of rule it has become an integral part of the Carthaginian world.

The island Kingdom of Trinacria came under Carthaginian protection at the invitation of House Barcelona of Aragon during a Pope-backed Neapolitan invasion of the island.  Thanks in no small part to the conspicuous valor of Libyan marines, the island fell under then-Tunisian control in its entirety.  Initially the noble hierarchies in Sicily were untouched and all that really changed for Sicilian Catholics was who they paid their taxes to, however this control was immediately contested.

In very short order the Aragonese betrayed their initial invitation and, together with the Kingdom of Castile and the Kingdom of Naples, they invaded the island again.  This time it was the Byzantine civilian fleet which ferried Tunisian forces to the island, leading to a successful defense of the island and the total defeat of Crusader forces and the expulsion of the House Barcelona nobility from Sicily.

Despite the decisive conclusion of the conflict, Sicily remained a flashpoint in the Mediterranean in the following decades, and despite this it prospered.  A single Naples-backed rebellion ultimately failed to seize the island.  Peace would reign over the island undisturbed until the Sardinian Conflict of 1404-1406 which saw a minor Neapolitan incursion at Palermo swiftly repulsed.

At this point the Sicilian people had become well accustomed to Carthaginian rule and prospered significantly, and the island was well on its way to becoming an integral part of the Carthaginian core territories.  Ushered in by the efforts of Queen Eirini Palaiologos, the people of the island saw the circumstance of their relationship with Carthage as no different than the relationship between the Sultan and his Byzantine Queen.  It was a marriage of two very different powers and very different people, but it worked.  The differences in the two complemented one another, commerce flowed, and people lived happy, productive lives.  It may have at first seemed unusual, but it worked.  Gradually the Sicilian people had come to see themselves not as a people apart, but as an integral part of the Carthaginian tapestry, and one with a vested interest in Carthaginian success.

By early 1424 the divisions between the two realms had become largely academic and more burdensome than any purpose they once served.  In July of that year these separations were abolished entirely, with the island of Sicily being fully integrated into the Carthaginian state.  A naval governorship was established in Messina to serve as the island's capital, in the very seat of power from where Ibrahim Hafsid, brother of the Sultan, had defended Sicily and rejected Papist posturing.  Now it was not only the defensive, but the administrative heart of Sicily as well.

## An Arrowshot East

The procession moved west and into the English Marches with solemn dignity. Nary was there a disturbance in those fields, for th
events.fullText:
## An Arrowshot East

The procession moved west and into the English Marches with solemn dignity. Nary was there a disturbance in those fields, for the lords regularly visited them and [made sure of it](https://discord.com/channels/507915886477312023/1449171134317723791/1519082651250720820). Into the domains of the Justiciar they progressed and life became more troubled. Still, the weight of holy protection, and the sword, [was with them](https://discord.com/channels/507915886477312023/1449171134317723791/1519082588759916544). There would be no disturbance to the ‘holy’ artifact once wielded by the adopted Queen of the Britons herself…*by the English*.

> "Yn wir, ni chewch fwy mentergar, mwy anwyl, na mwy nwyfus gamp na gweithredoedd Twm Siôn Cati. Ef a gymer oddi wrthych chwi, yr arglwyddi Saesneg, ac a raddfêl i'r bobl anghenus. Myn esgyrn Duw, y mae yn beth rhyfeddol!"

> "Certis, never shal ye fynde a more darynge, more darlynge, ne more dasshynge exployt than the dedis of Thomas John Catherine. He shal take from you, ye Englissh lordis, and gyve it to the nedy peple. By Goddes bones, hit is a mervailous thyng!"

A **Villan** of almost certainly Welsh extract **robbed** the [Abby of Strata Marcella](https://discord.com/channels/507915886477312023/1505545331591610549/1515787287068413962) during the night. He and his band of misfits made certain not to hurt the resident monks nor rob any of the actual valuables held by the royally patronized institution. Instead, only a solitary monk happened to see their intrusion and was promptly taken prisoner. He was discovered far later that morning tied up backwards on a mule which was pointed in the direction of England.

*The case has proven an [embarrassment to local authorities](https://discord.com/channels/507915886477312023/1449171134317723791/1519134990716244048). Little trail has been found, no witnesses have come forward despite some undoubtedly seeing the fiend, and the nearby exclusively Welsh area of Trefaldwyn - or Montgomery, has universally frustrated English attempts to find the perpetrators. If the ‘King of Brittania’s legitimacy’ still existed it was probably **legitimately** picking scraps out of a Welsh peasant's teeth at present.*

# The Red Succession Begins #
## Crisis of the Horizon ##

Since the ascension of King Charles I of Hungary in 1308, the Capetian house of Anjou has r
events.fullText:
# The Red Succession Begins #
## Crisis of the Horizon ##

Since the ascension of King Charles I of Hungary in 1308, the Capetian house of Anjou has ruled the Hungarian realm uninterrupted for nearly a hundred years, with the royal title being peacefully passed down through the Angevin kings of Hungary. However, the actions and events during the reign of Charles II de Anjou, third Angevin monarch to sit upon the Hungarian, have sown the seeds of a bloody harvest to come. When the fateful arrow pierced the heart of Crown Prince Robert de Anjou and he breathed his last, the seeds of what was to come had been sown. Those seeds had been watered and nurtured by the tears of the grieving king, who, in his attempts to have his children inherit Hungary, uprooted the Salic laws of succession and proclaimed his second daughter, Princess Johanna, heir to Hungary. Now as many sprouts have appeared as there are claimants to the lands of Hungary. With each day the old king grew older, and more so the red harvest grew.

Now the old king is dead, and now a Queen wears the Crown of Saint Stephen and sits upon the Hungarian throne. Although Queen Johanna has remarkably ruled Hungary peacefully and through a period of long stability, from France and Austria to Ruthenia and Constantinople, opportunistic and avaricious forces look on and prepare to seize their moment, either for the crown or personal gain. They wait in ambush, like wolves awaiting the perfect moment to fall upon their prey. Although many would deny it and blissfully preach that the realm is witnessing the dawn of a golden age, conflict is inevitable.

One may not be able to surmise who'd be the beneficiaries of this conflict or who may triumph over when the dust has settled, but one fact is certain. A bloody harvest will be reaped, a Red Succession shall engulf the land, and its touch shall affect all from common to noble.

> “And there went out another horse that was red: and power was given unto him that sat thereon to take peace from the earth, and that they should kill one another: and there was given unto him a great sword." 
-# Revelation 6:4

[[Thread Here]](https://discord.com/channels/507915886477312023/1519256016980672645)

<@302128861209165824> <@423689694258397195> <@190640828954181632> <@1432820281000333392> <@261290667106369536> <@493830084893999104> <@759277551117402194> <@460814348487753730> <@1123821809704374313> <@307587752860581888><@252560004069064705>

```This post and thread will start the Hungarian succession crisis, a crisis resulting from the actions of Angevin monarchs that started with the death of King Charles II's only son, Prince Robert, the uprooting of Salic Law in Hungary, and the proclamation of his second daughter Princess Johanna as heir. 

Until this point, the Hungarian realm has been seemingly living in a blissful Golden Age. That is going to end. 

The subsequent conflict for the Crown of St. Stephen between different claimants and their factions may not only result in the rise of a new ruling dynasty in Hungary, but also bring untold  death and tumult to the Hungarian realm that shall greatly shape the Kingdom’s destiny in the coming decades. 

Welcome to the Red Succession!```

events.snippet:
# Sardinia and Sicily

How can glory be to god, when the heathen mocks our shores with his claims and presence. Truly the greatest mistake of my life
events.fullText:
# Sardinia and Sicily

How can glory be to god, when the heathen mocks our shores with his claims and presence. Truly the greatest mistake of my life was not reclaiming my crown sooner, and doing what the meek Castilian is incapable of, this gold will build ships of war, this gold will train knights in the Order of Christ, and this gold will reclaim Sardinia upon my honor. Should I refuse the call to war from my peers within this life against the heathen, then call me godless, call me coward, and see a godly man ascend my throne. This aggression can not stand, and it shall kneel before the wrath of god, and I shall see it done in his name. <@297185126947880960> 

# A Letter to the Sultan

I do not know what manner of witchcraft you have used to bring the Romans under your spell, but you will find that us in Portugal are immune to such naivety. I see you for what you are, even from my throne hundreds of miles away I see your barbarism, and smell the infection of Christendom done with your hands and deeds. If you are a learned man, than heed this warning, you shall come to pay for your crimes unless you adhere to the demands of the faithful, and abandon your dominion over Sardinia. But in the case you are the rabid dog I imagine, know you shall be given the only cure to remedy your sickness. Death.

||<@302128861209165824> ||

events.snippet:
# Dinis De Borgonha - THE GREAT RESTORER

After decades of strife following the death of his Grandfather Alfonso, and a lasp of De Borgonha rule trigg
events.fullText:
# Dinis De Borgonha - THE GREAT RESTORER

After decades of strife following the death of his Grandfather Alfonso, and a lasp of De Borgonha rule triggered by his grandfather, King Dinis reasurted his family to the Crown of Portugal, and with it has brought about sweeping reforms, and ideas stemming from the English court which has brought extravagant change to Lisboa. 

For the first time in history thanks to new advances in ship craft and administration, trade flows to Lisboa from as far as Naples. This has created immense wealth in great contrast to that of previous Kings. 

As Dinis solidifies his reign, with vast support from the nobility whom had enabled his return, and the effects of his meticulous management, the wars of Portuguese Succession have nearly drawn to a close. With only one rivaling claimant left, the De Borgonha dynasty will either be entrenched as the chosen rulers of Portugal, or their influence will once again be confined to the corridors of the English court.

# Education for the Masses

However despite his rule being unilaterally popular, the clergy have become increasingly worried and displeased by the king’s proclivity to foreign ideas inspired by John Wycliff and [João das Regras.](https://discord.com/channels/507915886477312023/1505545331591610549/1517371485168074913) Though by no accounts a heretic, Dinis has become obsessed with the debate of such ideas, often corresponding with scholars in the University of Lisboa, and even some students. And despite council from his bishops, the King is disinterested in stamping out these ideas, or empowering the clergy to do so. However luckily for him, his Archbishop Pedro Couto, appears to also be interested in changing their minds through persuasion, rather than command.

Though some priests and bishops are less understanding, and begin to whisper dangerous words to their parishes.

# Henry The Navigator

From the humble Port of Portucale, a new admiral takes to the waves, descending from a humble noble family called house Avis, the young man is a talented and gifted sailor, who has become obsessed with observing, and understanding Aragonese ship design. Primarily how an armada can make use of such devastating firepower as seen on their ships. With Muslims dominating Christians across the Mediterranean, it has become paramount to the King that his fleet be able to secure Christian trade, and aid his friend in Aragon. <@1007414347347271730>

# The Order of Christ

With Saracens invading several Christian kingdoms, and the godless Fadrique on his border, King Dinis makes more and more of the royal treasury available to the Order of Christ. Long known to be renowned soldiers, it is his hope that their prowess and faith can guide the simple peasants should their pikes be needed in either the name of King, or Christ.

# Joao De Borgonha

King Dinis had grown up in England under the reign of Eleanor Plantagenet, she herself had given him reign to return home with English knights from his estates, and retake grandfather's throne. And from then until her death she had been a strong supporter of his reign despite the distance that separated them, and even let his brothers continue to live within her court, and govern the holdings gifted to his family in his stead. And the promise of eternal friendship between their courts was not something Dinis took lightly, and though Dinis is King in his own right, for most of his life, Eleanor was his Queen. 

Following the increasing unstable situation in England, Dinis sent word to his brother Joao, urging him to gauge the landscape, and find a match worthy of his son Afonso, and perhaps Joao himself. Among this ask was the insinuation to safeguard his houses interest, and see whatever happens, that their position in the court was not tainted, nor forsaken. 

Dear Brother

Even from this great distance it would take a blind man not to see the consequences unfolding in England, as the eldest member of our family, I trust you to see to our matters in the late Eleanor’s court. To this end I ask you to make a match with a suitable noblewoman of your choosing for my dear son, so our friendship may be protected. Though I do warn you, in the effort, do not risk what our father built, and think carefully before you set something in motion which you can not see through to a welcome end. In order to safeguard your protection in this time, I have ordered the knights of Coventry to gather near the docks, and they await ships to ferry them back to Coventry to be used at your disposal. Pay mind to that word, they are for your protection, not so you can play at war, act accordingly. 

Your brother, 

King Dinis De Borgonha and Count of Jersey.

||<@1106651545296195655> will try to figure out what i'm doing with this tomorrow night||

# The Road to Jerusalem is Open Again!

It took many years to restore stability to the Kingdom of Jerusalem after the Tenth Crusade. Tensions were hig
events.fullText:
# The Road to Jerusalem is Open Again!

It took many years to restore stability to the Kingdom of Jerusalem after the Tenth Crusade. Tensions were high upon the initial reconquest, and civil unrest sparked several revolts. By 1427 however, that unrest seemed to be settling down. Concessions granted to the Muslims and Eastern Christians by the Queen have erased fears of a tyrannical Catholic regime. Now, with the famed **Knights Hospitaller** agreeing to the terms offered to them by the Queen of Jerusalem, the Order will doubtlessly help much in further securing internal stability. For the Order is not only given the task of defending Antioch, but also to fulfill their old duties in the first Kingdom of Jerusalem. Free from their obligations to attack Christians in Italy or rule over vast lands in Anatolia, they now continued their original, sacred mission: **caring for the sick and defending the faith.** The roads of pilgrimage to Jerusalem are reinforced by these Holy Knights, helping ensure the safety of the travelers who take up the cross and venture to the great city.

So, at last the call goes out across Christendom: **The Road to Jerusalem is open again! All, whether high King or lowly peasant, are welcome to take up the sign of the Cross and go in safety to fulfill a holy pilgrimage in the Kingdom of Jerusalem!**

**(Every Christian ruler is free to take a pilgrimage to Jerusalem if they so wish! Make donations---or don't, just know that the faithful will be welcomed by the Queen and guarded by the Knights Hospitaller as you make your faithful journey to the land of Jesus Christ!)**

-# Pilgrams on the journey to Jerusalem

**(End)**

events.author: JPThunda
events.snippet:
**A Brazen Betrayal & Righteous Reprisal**


It began slowly at first.  Rumors of harassed merchant vessels in the Tyrhennean Sea, black-flagged vesse
events.fullText:
**A Brazen Betrayal & Righteous Reprisal**


It began slowly at first.  Rumors of harassed merchant vessels in the Tyrhennean Sea, black-flagged vessels prowling the coast of Sicily and the Maghreb.  Not enough to impact the flow of commerce in a truly meaningful way, more of an inconvenience at first, and thus it was slow to rise to the attention of the Sultan and his Queen.  When the island of Sicily began to suffer more serious disruption in their shipping and day to day travel, only then was the issue finally raised with the Sultan.

When knowledge of what was happening did reach the court, the Sultan ordered the mighty Carthaginian fleet to begin hunting down these pirate raiders.  For the most part however, Carthaginian vessels were large warships, and ill suited to diminishing piracy.  Ill suited, but not ill captained, for within a few months the pirates in the Maghrebi coast had been eradicated, with fully one quarter of their ships captured.  Their crews were aggressively interrogated before their executions, and what they revealed enraged the Sultan and sent a wave of shock rippling through the populace on Sicily.

These pirates, they claimed, were from a so-called Holy Order from far, far to the north, from the lands of the Norse, and how they had managed to come here was at the invitation and sponsorship of the Pope in Lyon and the French Crown.  They had not sailed here–French shipwrights had built vessels for them, supplied them, and in some cases crewed them.  They were given payment and clear orders to attack any ships flying under Carthaginian flags–including the ships of Sicilian and Sardinian Catholics.  Of course they had far exceeded this mandate, as is the want of brigands and thieves, and preyed upon the peoples of Italy from Genoa to Napoli, attacking merchant vessels indiscriminately.  They preyed upon shipping as far away as the Aegean too, where the Roman Empire had managed to curtail them.

The Sultan was outraged.  Had he not offered support to the Lyonese against the Papal Tyrant in Rome?  And to engage in this sort of attack in so cowardly a way, attacking with no declaration of intent, no talks, only blind, indiscriminate violence and acts of uninhibited avarice and greed.  That was a vile act in its own right, but as much as the Sultan was angered by the complete lack of decorum and abdication of decency, the people of Sicily and Sardinia were affected even more-so, for they had borne the brunt of these pirate attacks, and they had been betrayed.

From the outset of the schism between France and the Papacy, the Catholics of Sicily had supported Lyon.  Indeed their lobbying, in part, had been what convinced the Sultan to offer his support to Lyon and France.  But now?  Betrayed and attacked by the men who claimed they had been sent to save.  Preyed upon mercilessly by their would-be liberators.  For over eighty years, every time a foreign Catholic had come to Sicily it had been as a conqueror, and now they came as pirates and raiders.  And they claimed it was on Christ's behalf?!  It was too much–one stone too many, one bridge too far–and the outrage could no longer be borne.

On Sicily the people lashed out at the Catholic institutions, and a mob stormed the properties of the Catholic diocese on the island.  The sad truth is that those clergy on the island were victims just as much as the laity, but they became the objects of their ire nonetheless.  The Sultan's forces stationed on the island did what they could to quell the unrest, but they could not be everywhere.  Only once Queen Eirini came to the island, visiting its many cities and towns in turn, did the violence abate.  Turning their back on the Catholics who had turned their backs on them, a wave of conversion swept over the Sicilian Catholics–not to Islam, at least not for most, but to Orthodox Christianity.  To the religion of the Queen who had seen them, walked among them, advocated for them, and made them feel heard.

On Sardinia, where the Sultanate had only recently seized control, the response was more mixed.  Those who suffered directly at the hands of the pirates were outraged and felt personally attacked.  Those who were inconvenienced by shortages and disruptions of commerce were likewise vocally opposed to the pirates, but there was a minority who supported the actions.  Those who had not benefited as much from the Sultanate's rule, particularly in the northern portions of the island where they were still more beholden unto Roman merchants than Carthaginian ones, saw the most support for the pirates.  It is a sad irony that these people were more impacted by the piracy than their southern comrades, where the Carthaginian patrols curtailed the pirate's efforts.

This attack demanded a response, and one which would not be delivered meekly.  Already the Sultan had issued an ultimatum and demanded the piracy cease, but these demands had been ignored.  Now was the time to make good on those threats, and to go further still.  The Sultan bore the anger and indignation of all of his people, and his armies and navies would be the instrument of their vengeance's deliverance.

**The Sultan's Declaration**
Date:1424-07-01][Country: Carthage][Location:Carthage][Tag:WarDec]

*To the Pirates of Visby, wretched and cowardly, the Papacy in Lyon, faithless and ungrateful, the King of France, who sponsors and enables this vile perfidy, and all others who render aid, support, and comfort to these northern brigands, thieves, and vikings, I make this declaration:*

*In retaliation for the brazen, cowardly, undeclared attacks on Carthaginian commerce and the trade of the Mediterranean, the Carthaginian fleet shall patrol and privateer the regions of the Tyrhennean Sea, the Gulf of Valencia, and the Adriatic Sea.  Any Catholic vessels in this area flagged by Catholic realms not in good standing with the Carthaginian Sultanate shall be subject to interdiction and seizure of their cargos.  The crews of any vessels found to be participating in the enterprise of piracy against Carthaginian vessels shall be subject to seizure, arrest, and penalties of indentured servitude.  Any coastal communities supporting the enterprise of piracy are likewise subject to punitive raids to destroy the havens of these pirates and bring these criminals to Carthaginian justice.*

*Should the piracy by the would-be vikings of Visby be ended, then these privateering actions shall likewise cease.  If the piracy sponsored by Lyon continues, then further actions will be taken to enforce safety and security of commerce within the Mediterranean.*

*So declared by the illustrious and magnificent Sultan Bahir Hafsid, Sultan of Carthage, King of Trinacria, King of Sardinia, Sheik of Algiers…*

events.snippet:
# The Works of Ibn Khaldun:


Following the great thinker’s death in 1406, much of his thoughts and ideas have been propagated throughout the Muslim w
events.fullText:
# The Works of Ibn Khaldun:


Following the great thinker’s death in 1406, much of his thoughts and ideas have been propagated throughout the Muslim world, with many of his ideas taking root in the Sultanate of Morocco and those under its aegis.

One of his most famous works currently discussed is his series of tomes called the  Kitāb al-ʻIbar which many including the great sultan of Morocco can claim to own a complete collection of the 7 books. These books have many differing thoughts in them but also function as a history of the different dynasties and peoples of the north African area. These histories of both the maghrib but also the world use many sources and are perhaps the most complete record of some of these regions many would see. It makes learning the history of the maghrib and its people much simpler for those that perhaps know nothing outside religious dogma of the area, aswell as making the people there and their lives more relatable for outsiders.

Book one known as Al-Muqaddima is the most famous of the 7 books and the most unique. While the others function mostly as a guide to world history as known in the Muslim world, Al-Muqaddima goes into many of the inner workings of empires, and their function. The most controversial idea in the  Al-Muqaddima focuses on the idea of the rise and fall of dynastic empires in an almost predicable cycle. That all dynasties start as an outsider or “barbarian” before usurping the power of the previous dynasty. That upon doing so, they would inevitably assimilate to or usurp the systems and luxury of the old and eventually stagnate upon reaching a hight and becoming complacent. This complacency is followed by a period of weakening allowing a new barbarian dynasty to step in continuing the cycle. 

These books also contain the idea of Asabiyyah. This is the idea of the existence of a social cohesion around smaller groups like family units, local communities and tribal groups, which when combined with religion like Islam can be magnified. This bond is said to exist in all forms of civilisation but is most powerful in smaller groups and becomes frayed in larger more developed civilisations. That when this occurs it can be replaced by a differing or more condensed Asabiyyah, contributing to how dynasties rise and fall.

His works also go into the economies of empires and how they are run. He writes that things like poverty of the masses are not to do with just poor managing of coin but instead are mostly affected by external sources out with their control and thus it falls to the sultans and rulers to become involved in dealing with their causes. He also believed that as in the sultanate of Morrocco all coin should have a intrinsic value and be based off gold and silver rather than a barter economy used by tribes or the copper-based coins used in Europe. 

His works also go into religion and scientific realms. He believed that all sciences could be divided into those of the Quran and those without such as arithmetic and astrology and that both had vital importance. He argued that the basis of all successful sociates was a foundation in education in both types of sciences, something that recently has caught on in Morroco with the expansion of libraries and the opening of the grand university in Fez. In terms of religion his works push the belief that the human spirit is the mediator between Allah and humanity, which makes the human spirit both immortal in theory can communicate with Allah directly. Yet it also states that most human spirits have lost this ability making them only connected to the sensory world and the very few that do not are those classed as prophets chosen by Allah to speak to him and receive his commands. His belief was that if one concentrated hard enough he could see beyond the sensory world if only for a moment and that in doing so one could become attached to what they saw, using this as the basis for most pagan religions. That a selfless man that is good in faith for faith alone, rather than the desire to rise above others, then he could come closer to Allah and the ability to communicate. Others that try but are not selfless instead will consort with daemons.

<@297185126947880960> <@298192682554228738> <@422498641425596418> <@1007414347347271730> <@234699731715096576> <@302128861209165824> (Anyone i imagine would have heard of it or be influenced by on some level)

events.snippet:
** THE EDICT OF GIBRALTAR **

With the recent large scale uptick in piracy within the confines of the Mediterranean resulting in open conflict between
events.fullText:
** THE EDICT OF GIBRALTAR **

With the recent large scale uptick in piracy within the confines of the Mediterranean resulting in open conflict between the Sultanate of Carthage and Visby, who is backed by several significant Catholic powers, the importance of the straight of Gibraltar has become paramount.

Thusly, King Fadrique of Castile has ordained that any seafaring vessel attempting to pass through the straight of Gibraltar that is flying the black flag or does not clarify their sovereignty shall be requested to dock in the port at Tarifa as to properly identify themselves and their purpose. Failure to comply will result in said vessel being sunk.

This will have no bearing upon the travels of ships that clearly identify themselves as belonging to a sovereign power, as long as there is no suspicion that said identification is false.

We hope the message is clear: Castile shall clamp down upon piracy in all its forms, regardless of whomever said pirates work for.

If a vessel cannot prove their mission legitimate, they will be turned back whence they came.

<@327561459976372224> <@297185126947880960> <@298192682554228738> <@1007414347347271730> <@150411715757080576> <@234699731715096576> <@315868897012219904> <@302128861209165824> <@698400527834415145> <@326414282851876875>

|| Ostensibly, this is an absolute and immutable decree. However, perhaps some "exceptions" can be made, if a few coins fall from an enterprising captain's purse from time to time. Nothing in the official edict mentions anything of this sort, of course. But the Royal officials stationed in the province of Cadiz are aware of the unspoken rules, simply waiting for someone to inquire. Discussions about the creation of false flags and identification can be made, and each transaction will be made as a one-time affair. (Pay me each time, it will be a different amount each time as well). And, of course, secrecy is key. No records shall be kept.||

<@327561459976372224> <@297185126947880960> <@326414282851876875>

||# An envoy in the night||


||An envoy is dispatched towards the Castilian court. He carries little but only to give the king of castile a message from the young sultan. ||

||"There are many that have worked for years to undermine just rule in these lands with there greed and division. As the guardians of the pillars it falls to us to decide who shall enter and who shall leave. Perhaps it would be better for both our peoples if we worked on that together."||

<@422498641425596418>

events.author: Bas1leus
events.snippet:
|| ** The Shadows Respond ** ||



|| The envoy receives no reply, or even acknowledgment of his presence. Disheartened, he plans his leave, taking si
events.fullText:
|| ** The Shadows Respond ** ||



|| The envoy receives no reply, or even acknowledgment of his presence. Disheartened, he plans his leave, taking silence as his answer. However, on the night before his departure, a man approaches him wordlessly, beckoning him to a conference room, lit only by a single candle on the table. Once both are seated, the shadowy man speaks in hushed tones. ||

|| "Let us be swift. I speak on behalf of His Majesty King Fadrique." *He presents a royal seal as proof.* "He has heard your proposal, but he cannot be seen to acknowledge it. However, he is heartened by this outreach. He believes monitoring both sides of the straight alone is too difficult for the investment required. Some things would eventually slip through the cracks. It would be beneficial if the Moroccan Sultanate would assist and make this a joint endeavor. Sadly, at the present time, any form of agreement with an African sovereign would cause mass outrage within the Catholic world. This is why this must stay a secret. Take this news back to the Sultan, and send a man. Have him carry this, and I will find him in the way I found you." *He pushes forward a unique seal.* "We never met." ||

|| *He snuffs out the candle, and fades back into the darkness.* ||

<@327561459976372224>

||# A Sultans Reaction||



||When the envoy returns he walks up to the young Sultan requesting permission to approach and speak to his excellence directly. Upon doing so he whispers into the sultans ear. No one in the court hears what he says yet all notice when the sultan smiles and laughs softly. He nods and beckons his most loyal retainer giving him instructions few hear but makes all nobles feel on edge, the younger man having gained a reputation of curbing their long held excess. It is said the sultan has a smile on his face the rest of the day, but none know of the set up being created in the background and the undercover deal agreed.||
