events.snippet:
# The Spread of Ideas

Dinis De Borgonha is a renowned scholar, both hated and loved for his approval of learning unorthodox ideas on philosophy and t
events.fullText:
# The Spread of Ideas

Dinis De Borgonha is a renowned scholar, both hated and loved for his approval of learning unorthodox ideas on philosophy and theology within his Universities. One of his core beliefs becoming that so long if his faith is true, then it is incorruptible by the ideals of others. So that learning could not impose damnation, but only serve as a chance to convince others of his truth, and to expand the way in which those in his court see the Christian world. Essentially meaning that the spread of ideas was not something to fear, but a natural process of enhancing ones mind and faith. 

Therefore after his actions in opening the schools of Lisboa, repealing religious censorship, and not impeding the spread of philosophy stemming from Islamic scholars, his courtiers could hardly feign shock when he arranged for his court priest to visit Morocco with a heap of gold, bearing his seal. 

 *In January of 1427, a priest arrives in Ar-Rabat, bearing a letter for the Sultan 'Abd al-'Aziz II, along with a small guard and a small chest which clinks with each of his servants steps.*

|| <@327561459976372224> do you accept this man into Morocco?||

events.author: Guy
events.snippet:
# JUST TRY AND COMPARE MY FAIRNESS,

# TO THE FOOL WHO STILL DENIES IT.

*Seljuk diplomats at first, high off the coat tails of victory, pushed hard f
events.fullText:
# JUST TRY AND COMPARE MY FAIRNESS,

# TO THE FOOL WHO STILL DENIES IT.

*Seljuk diplomats at first, high off the coat tails of victory, pushed hard for large territorial concessions from the ancient Empire. With the goal to sieze one of their key economic hubs within the Northern Cities. Moreover such a peace would sever the connection between Constantinople and the mainland, or atleast make the toll far less navigable.*

*However the realities oftue situation soon made themselves clear, the Byzantines refused to give land unless taken, which however fair on their end put a hard stop on any ideas to sieze land. While the Turks could certaintly cobtinue their march, and moreover destroy Byzantine presence in Anatolia should they choose, that risked a very unfortunate outcome: the Cathaginians.*

*Carthage had not yet directly involved itself in the war, yet paranoia as well as simple logic would say that Carthage would send it's armies, even if not in full, over to Anatolia, and then defeat would soon be grasped from the jaws victory. That is without mentioning the economic strain this war had put the Sultanate under, with the previously talked about debt and inflation ever increasing.*

*Thus negotiations moved away from the siezing of land, and towards monetary compensation, the Byzantines were demanded all the money that they could, which to the Sultans knowledge was roughly 800 ducats.*

*It was a small sum for a war that could've seen the end of Turkic reign in Anatolia, and it was expected by the Sultan that the Byzantines would remember his generosity in war. He doubted it though, and he knew that he could not afford a paupers peace next time. He would make sure, whether by land or blood, whether wars won or lost, the Romans will pay when they next march, that another generation may be lost in the  mountains, and that even if they find illucid victory, it will be stained.*

-# IT'S COMING.

events.author: ST Peter
events.snippet:
# The End of the Lusignans

Little was initially expected of Guy de Lusignan, but when he came to the Holy Land in the 1170's, he quickly rose to prom
events.fullText:
# The End of the Lusignans

Little was initially expected of Guy de Lusignan, but when he came to the Holy Land in the 1170's, he quickly rose to prominence. After marrying Sibylla, the sister of King Baldwin IV of Jerusalem, he soon found himself being made the King after Baldwin IV's death. From him spawned the Lusignan dynasty, Kings of Jerusalem, and Lords of Cyprus. Through the decades, and then the centuries they had ruled over lands in the Catholic far east. It was its own series of ups and downs, and the dynasty produced its fair share of good Kings and bad Kings. Now in the early 15th century, the Lusignans do not seem to be destined to rule for much longer. For despite King Peter I de Lusignan's great triumph of seeing the Kingdom of Jerusalem restored, his reign would be the beginning of the end of the old dynasty.

Through a series of unfortunate deaths, Peter I had no sons, but he did have a daughter, the Princess Catherine de Lusignan. In his own selfishness, Peter I chose her to be his heir apparent instead of one of his brothers, for he cared little for them. And, for the interests of the Kingdom, Peter I chose not to marry her off to some local nobility or distant branch of the Lusignans, but instead to the Crown Prince of a distant but powerful Kingdom. When Peter I died in 1411, Catherine was anointed as Queen of Jerusalem, Cyprus, and Cilicia, and her husband, Crown Prince Fadrique de Trastámara, was anointed as King of Jerusalem, Cyprus, and Cilicia. Soon, Fadrique's own father would die, and thus the Union of the Kingdoms of Jerusalem, Cyprus, Cilicia, Castile, Portugal, and Leon would begin in earnest. This great union, however, had to come at the cost of the Lusignan ruling dynasty.

There may indeed be Lusignans still out there, serving in the court and mayhaps even conspiring for their own claims to the throne, but as it stands now the dynasty, in name at least, will no longer be Kings or Queens. Though the blood of the founder, Guy de Lusignan, may still run through the veins of the future Kings of the Christian east, it is certainly the dawn of a new age, with a Castillian Monarch ruling over the most sacred of lands.

In any case, even though the Spanish monarch may rule in name, it is the nobility who will have the true control of the old Lusignan Kingdoms, for their power only continues to grow and solidify. It is unlikely that the future King of Jerusalem, Cilicia, and Cyprus will be able to exercise much authority over the region for decades...if not centuries to come. For the nobility, made up partly of the remaining Lusignans, will not be quick to follow the decrees that a King many hundreds of leagues away enacts. They are a prideful bunch, much like Guy de Lusignan himself was, many centuries ago...

-# Guy de Lusignan, founder of the dynasty

**(End)**

<@422498641425596418>

events.author: Ceb
events.snippet:
## Cŵn Annwn - The Hounds of Annwn

***Barking** wakes up the old man in the middle of the night.*

Those damn dogs were at it again. He had told Dafy
events.fullText:
## Cŵn Annwn - The Hounds of Annwn

***Barking** wakes up the old man in the middle of the night.*

Those damn dogs were at it again. He had told Dafydd last week to shut them up at such hours or he’d do it for him. Though as the old man looked outside he thought better of the notion. The cold winter winds were coming down from Cadair Idris and reminding him, nearly as sternly as his wife, that it would do no good.

> “Myn uffern.”
> -# “By hell.”
*He cursed with some venom into his pillow.*

The old man’s legs were cold and the fire in the nearby stove was out by now. It would take some time before he could fix either. He now doubted if he’d get a good rest courtesy of that layabout neighbor and their disruptive beasts. Couldn’t they just keep them locked up inside like the rest of the village?

*The barkings drew further apart, then **stopped**. The old man breathed a sigh of relief.*

Some time passed and the old man had a small fire going. It was pleasant, and strange, for the only sound around him now was the occasional snapping of twigs in the heat. There was nothing…no wind, no snoring from the nearby bed, not even a strangely reassuring bark. It was dead silent but for his own actions.

*His ears were **pierced** by a howl that shook his very bones.*

The old man involuntarily flinched in its striking interruption. Wolves, most likely, and large ones at that. He knew how to deal with them and quickly moved to grab his trusty longbow. It wouldn’t be practical if they got close, but all he needed to do was sight the pack leader once and it’d be done.

*No one else moved. No one else seemed to hear the horrid howl. The village remained the very image of stillness…at least amongst its humanity.*

A flash of white glanced past his hovel’s small window. Several more, followed by a glinting of red ensued. The wind began again and there was again not a sound but the soft paddings of paws on a hardened winter’s soil. Even the fire seemed to shrink. The old man then slowly looked up and did not believe; he prayed but could not comprehend the dreadfully merciful visage of **Gwyn ap Nudd** as the figure commanded the door to reveal the old man without ever opening. Endlessly short moments then passed and the old man could not compel himself to move no matter how hard he tried. He could not even cry out as the jaws of Arawn’s hound closed around his heart without ever piercing his chest.

*An old man died on the slopes of the Chair of Idris: kindling at his side, cross in his hand, and a look of relief and horror on his face. His heart had stopped, **and the ride of the Wild Hunt had just begun across Britain**.*

events.author: Reehaw
events.snippet:
## *Hallowed Be Thy Name*

To Jan Hus, the [invitation of Geoffrey de Dampierre](https://discord.com/channels/507915886477312023/1505545415213449216/1
events.fullText:
## *Hallowed Be Thy Name*

To Jan Hus, the [invitation of Geoffrey de Dampierre](https://discord.com/channels/507915886477312023/1505545415213449216/1518418039291379772) was poorly veiled. Even in that heart, had it believed truthfully in the words sealed in that letter bearing his name, the Priest of the Bethlehem Chapel knew well the character of Jobst Gothan. He knew, as well, the ties between Trier and Lyon, or rather, its puppet master in Paris. Knowing these, and having been besmirched much with the antagonism of Pope Gregory XII, which saw his first excommunication on behalf of Wycliffe’s teachings, and again excommunicated beneath Pope Nicholas IV on account of his own teachings, the men who called Hus their faithful companion questioned his choice to depart. Perhaps it held root in why Hus chose to enter the clergy in the first place: to develop his name. His desire for a life of comfort, however, had seemed to pass some years ago as his opposition to Rome and its powerful henchmen only grew. Or, perhaps, that young boy, sent off to the monastery at the mere age of ten, had blossomed truly into a Christian of olde, who feared not the lambasts and judgement of mortal men. 

His faithful friend, his predecessor even, the acclaimed and infamous Jeroným Pražsky, urged him not to depart knowing what it may entail. When this plea was first denied, he made another, that should Hus come under the hand of the vengeful Papal party that he himself will come forth to his aid. This too was denied, and Hus elaborated for his reason why.

> “I confide altogether in the all-powerful God, in my savior; He will accord me His Holy Spirit to fortify me in his truth, so that I may face with courage temptations, prison, and if necessary a cruel death.”

Perhaps what enticed him so was precisely the opposing ground he was invited to preach upon. Would it be fitting solely to give sermons to his countrymen, and not present his case before the Heart of Corruption, the Agents of a Dying Church, which they pluck and plunder? Hus did not claim to foretell the future, but he knew what awaited him, and he went towards it regardless. 

The liberty promised to him by Geoffrey was indeed afforded upon his arrival, but the spectacle of his lectures, and the ferocity of his words as a man twice-excommunicated and condemned, drew attention without pause. Even had Gothan not already sent for the agents of Pope Nicholas IV to receive the heresy of Hus directly, they would’ve come regardless. For two decades Hus had been a preacher untouched, condemned and challenged, whose presence and absence in Praha both inflamed tensions, and so it were that a man like him lectured on the [Eucharist, on the authority of scripture over Church hierarchy, and most impressively his lambasts of the practice of indulgences, that pilfering monetary engine which sustained the Church’s society around him](https://discord.com/channels/507915886477312023/1505545415213449216/1518353760252854374). It was an event that could do naught but draw attention, and leverage the eyes of the Church, her bishops, and her theologians to Trier.

That spectacle could only last so long. Jobst had intended to seal his fate from the start, and certainly had let him go on so much to dampen his name, yet not long enough to truly make his case amidst the rumors and accusations against him. The Papal party, as it arrived, brought at its helm a figure of great renown, of personal knowledge of Hus. A villain, so to speak, whose ripples in the court at Lyon since the flight of the Bavarian Nation from the University of Praha, have ingratiated himself with the papal court and further inflamed their cause against the ‘Wycliffite heretic from Bohemia.’ **Augustus Böhmer**, a former teacher of renown at Praha, whose prestigious word and defense of the Catholic Church had been the shield which Hus railed against until the intervention of Karel II. His arrival to Trier sealed the fate of Hus, for now a personal enemy of his arrived to marshal the attention of Christendom, at least more than it already had, to bear its full righteous fury upon his heresy, in lieu of his failure to do so in 1409.

On the twenty-sixth day of Hus’ attendance at Trier, not long after the arrival of Böhmer, he was finally seized and arrested. He was initially entrusted to the care of the clerk of the High Cathedral of Saint Peter in Trier, before being moved to residence to a prison along the bank of the Moselle, where the sewage of the nearby monastery hung over him with a pestilential air. It was not long before he grew ill with fever from his conditions, which so viciously attacked him that he almost passed before his trial—disallowing that, however, physicians were charged by the papal party to nurse him. 

The news of his imprisonment lent fire to the kindling across Bohemia, and even across religious boundaries the nobility united in opposition to the treatment of their countryman. Certainly, the treachery of the act made it all the more volatile: the Archbishop of Trier promised safety, and he allowed the Pope’s agents to imprison him regardless. The gentry found in themselves a voice, which they claimed to represent the will of the nation, and sent their urging to Geoffrey de Dampierre to release Hus, to appeal to his honor and justice, and to remind him of his promise. Geoffrey was, however, overruled even in his own land. He was told that the greater good of the Church overruled his promise, and that no formality of his could obstruct the deliverance of justice upon a heretic. In the voice of the assembled Church, Geoffrey was delivered the voice of God, allegedly, and stepped aside to deliver Jan Hus to his enemies.

The body of the Church gathered in Trier, and at last, His Holiness Nicholas IV made attendance himself while Hus languished in his prison. Jeroným Pražśky, having heard the news of his friend’s condition, and in disregard of Hus’ wishes, left Bohemia soon upon the revelation and made his way towards Trier. The charges against Hus, consecrated by the party of **Augustus Böhmer** and other papal legates, were brought before his pitiful state in the riverbank prison, where his requests for an advocate were denied. He would have to represent himself. After they finished their preparations, Hus was finally summoned for his trial on the 5th of July, 1425.

As he was brought before the gathered assembly, a body unified in the authority of the Pope, and not in the conciliar tradition as Würzburg had been, clanked with iron chains, Hus had his books presented to him. Questioned by the authority of Böhmer, Hus answered truthfully. The writings were his, he declared, when they asked. The articles of his crimes, so declared by the Church, were read next. Some covered truthful perceptions of his preachings, and naturally, others contained perversions and exaggerations, doctored perhaps by the hands of Böhmer and Gothan, among others. Hus attempted to clarify, to state what words were his, and what were subversive fabrications which he never taught, but the clamor of the hall drowned him out. When the tumult subsided, he once again ventured in his defense, but his eloquence, strained by his imprisonment, was once more drowned by the jeers of the faithful around him, who found the allegations against him dire and horrific. Amongst those defining the crowd’s levied assault upon him, was His Holiness himself. 

> “He is dumb, the pitiful goose squawks no more!”

Hus answered in return that he was silent, because he could not be heard among the noise. This cycle, however, did not break so easily. And the first day faded thus, with its successor bearing no greater fruit for Hus’ labors. Whensoever he had the opportunity to defend himself while clad in his chains, surrounded by armed men who themselves were surrounded by the body of his peers, he did so. He refuted Wycliffe’s stance on transubstantiation, he did not outright deny the divine institution of the papacy and members of the Church’s hierarchy, but still he remained stubborn that the efficacy of their official acts was dependent upon their spiritual character, moreso than their office. 

It did not matter that his arguments presented that he was in truth not quite that different to his condemners, for he had broken the greatest of bonds they desired for him: the bond of submission. He rejected total papal infallibility before the seat of Nicholas IV, and held no wound in his conscience for having broken those shackles. Enfeebled, ill, and weakening day by day by way of his material conditions, Hus still refused to recant and kneel in obeisance to the gathered fathers of the Church, even at the foot of the Pope. Even when exhorted by doctors sympathetic to his plight to simply acknowledge what the Council of Trier deemed true, regardless of its validity or not, so that he may be released to his followers at home, Hus resisted. It was there that, such a man who had entered the clergy for his own worldly desires of status and comfort, found sufficiently a strength within him to bear the assault of the mob, to suffer the burden of the accusations against him, and to render his submission non-achievable. 

Through the assistance of the nobles whom accompanied him to Trier, such as the renowned Jan Kepka z Chlum, Hus still wrote letters addressing his students at Praha, and the city at large, from his imprisonment when he felt up for the task. Chlum embraced him as a friend from his return of the day of trial, which Hus would write to his friends back home of the joy which it provided:

> “Oh, what a consolation to me, in the midst of my trials, to see that excellent nobleman stretch forth the hand to me, miserable heretic, languishing in chains, and already condemned by everyone.”

The time for his condemnation finally came, after a tumultuous trial in which he was afforded little words to his defense. To the chagrin of the Council, and to the soaring joy of Hus, he still retained friends and allies among the increasingly expanding body gathered at Trier. Most vocal from that small throng was **Paweł Włodkowic**, a Polish rector from the University of Kraków who took the time before the Pope to argue his case against the Teutonic Order, and against the conquests of pagan peoples in general, arguing from scripture for the rights of non-Christians to their lands and their freedom from slaughter. In that vein, he came to His’ defense when very few would, as he argued against the ability of the Church to so easily declare a heretic, and thus, condemn a man to death for defying the hierarchy of the Church. His words were not wholly revolutionary, and he attributed his position to the pacifism in the Bible, to the righteousness in turning the other cheek versus the act of execution. Despite those voices which defended him, the delegates of the Pope’s party, representing Böhmer and Gothan even, delivered a document of abjuration of Hus’ teachings, and for his submission to the Church, which they delivered to him in his cell. His friends were employed to implore him to agree to the terms to spare his life, yet Hus only agreed to abjure those teachings which he was wrongly accused of. 

> “I would rather be cast into the sea with a mill-stone about my neck, than offend those little ones to whom I preached the Gospel, by abjuring it.”

Hus, presented the temptation to bow his head before his accusers, to give into their demands so that his life may be spared, did not relent. For if he bowed his head before them, at the terms they demanded, he could never lift it again before his followers, before his own conviction and conscience, or before Christ his Lord. In the relief he found in choosing his fate, he wrote to his friends at home;

> “I write this letter in prison, and with my fettered hand, expecting my sentence of death tomorrow. When, with the assistance of Jesus Christ, we shall meet again in the delicious peace of the future life, you will learn how merciful God has shown himself towards me—how effectually he has supported me in the midst of my temptations and trials.”

> “I firmly hope that this life of Christ, which I engraved on men’s hearts at Bethlehem when I preached His Word, will not be effaced; and that after I have ceased to live it will be still better shown forth, by mightier preachers, to the great satisfaction of the people, and to my own most sincere joy, when I shall be again permitted to announce His Gospel—that is, when I shall rise from the dead.”

And so, on the Sixth of July, 1425, the stage was set—literally, a makeshift assemblage had been prepared for the Council specifically for these final proceedings. The majesty of the Church, of the Christian World, awaiting to deliver judgement to a ***haeresiarcha***, on full grandiose display. The Archbishop of Trier, Geoffrey, collected Hus from his cell, certainly an awkward affair, and delivered him before the doors of the church; where he awaited the completion of Mass inside, denied to him. Hus was taken to a raised platform and seated, where he may be viewed by the whole of the assembled body, and then presented before the Council, a bishop delivered a sermon against heresy and schism, which was finished in the plea to Nicholas IV, motioning as well towards Hus;

> “Destroy heresies and errors, but chiefly, that obstinate heretic!”

The accusations against Hus were read once more before him, and he refused to abjure. And so, the sentence came, condemnation as a heretic, as if it had not been decided before the trial began. Then, following that pronouncement, came a ceremony of degradation. He was dressed in priestly vestments, sacred in his appeal thus, and deposited into his hands a chalice, and asked if he would abjure now.

> “With what face, then, should I behold the heavens? How should I look on those multitudes of men to whom I have preached the pure gospel? No; I esteem their salvation more than this poor body, now appointed unto death.”

Following his refusal, they took the chalice once more from his hands.

> “O accursed Judas, who, having abandoned the counsels of peace, have taken part in that of the Jews, we take from you this cup filled with the blood of Jesus Christ.”

The significance in this measure was not lost upon Hus or the crowd, certainly, but it would definitely not be missed by his followers at home in Bohemia, and the denial of the chalice to Hus would be an engrained story in the cause of the Calixtines. Further remembered still, would be his response.

> “I hope, by the mercy of God, that this very day I shall drink of his cup in his own kingdom; and in one hundred years you shall answer before God and before me.”

Bishops ascended the platform and undressed him from those ecclesiastical garments he had been draped in, each one a curse and act of deposition to him as a man of the Church, and in their final act—the removal of his tonsure—they bickered over the manner in which to perform it, be it scissors or a blade. They decided upon the scissors, and cut about his tonsure, defacing him and by canon law removing him from the priesthood, before proceeding to place upon his head a cap, adorned in depictions of demons, and sprawled across it the text “Arch-Heretic.”

> “Most joyously will I wear this crown of shame for thy sake, O Jesus, who for me didst wear a crown of thorns.”

The bishops thus declared Hus’ soul for the devil, and delivered him into the hands of the Kaiser, the cruel **Giovanni Maria Visconti**, who in customary fashion, transferred Hus to **Ferdinand Maria von Wittelsbach**, the Count Palatine and highest vicar in Germany beneath the Kaiser, who would then bestow the heretic to the chief magistrate of Trier to see through his sentence. This affair was to keep the hands of the Church untainted, free from Hus’ death: they merely condemned him a heretic, and left him to the secular authorities to dispense their punishments. 

The city’s sergeants escorted Hus to his execution, the princes, prelates, and bishops following him in an amassed cavalcade. Their procession crossed a bridge, the Moselle beneath them, and proceeded to a meadow outside the city gates. There, Hus sank to his knees and recited the penitential psalms, as his stake was driven into the ground. He was seized and tied to the stake with ropes, and fastened to it by a chain which cast around his throat. The base which he stood was made from bundles of sticks, packed in between them straw as kindling, so as to ensure the spectacle would proceed. Wood was piled further up, some even reaching to his chin. The Chief Magistrate of Trier, compelled by the gaze of the Count Palatine, came up to Hus and implored, even now, to recant and renounce his errors so that his life may be spared.

> “What errors shall I renounce? I know myself guilty of none. I call God to witness that all that I have written and preached has been with the view of rescuing souls from sin and perdition; and, therefore, most joyfully will I confirm with my blood that truth which I have written and preached.” 

Hus’ words with men thus came to their close. As the straw beneath him was lit, and the fire blazed upwards, Hus had no words to shout, to plead for his life. Alone, he reserved his voice for God.

He began to sing.

events.author: ST Peter
events.snippet:
# The Crusader States and Byzantium: An Unusual Relationship.

The Eastern Roman Empire <@302128861209165824>  and the Crusader States of the Levant h
events.fullText:
# The Crusader States and Byzantium: An Unusual Relationship.

The Eastern Roman Empire <@302128861209165824>  and the Crusader States of the Levant have always had a complex relationship with each other. It was only thanks to Byzantine pleas for western aid that the First Crusade even began in the first place, though its scale was immediately beyond whatever the Byzantines had initially expected. At first the leaders of the Princes Crusade cooperated with the Emperor Alexios I, but after the Romans abandoned the Crusaders to their apparent doom in the long and costly siege of Antioch, the crusaders decided to forge their own path.

After the First Crusade's successful conclusion, relations with the Empire would become rather complex over the following decades. At times the resentment grew, like when Emperor John II Komnenos tried to seize Antioch from Prince Raymond of Poitiers, but was thwarted by a popular revolt in the city. Though at other times the Romans exercised some authority over the crusader states, and even cooperated jointly with them, like when King Amalric of Jerusalem married the grand-niece of Emperor Manuel I and then the two attempted to conquer Egypt, though this quickly ended in a failure.

Eventually the Byzantine's began to collapse, and Richard the Lionheart seized Cyprus from the pretender Isaac Komnenos in 1191. Soon afterwards the Fourth Crusade destroyed the Empire, and Saladin destroyed the Kingdom of Jerusalem. Interactions between the surviving crusader states in the levant and the Empire thus ended, and even when the Laskarids and Palaiologoi retook Constantinople from the Latin Empire in 1261, both the Empire and the barely surviving Kingdom of Jerusalem were too weak and focused on their own local regions to ever have any real desire to interact diplomatically with one another.

This only really changed in 1337, when Jerusalem and the rest of the mainland crusader states had fallen to the Mamluks and only Lusignan Cyprus remained. The heir to the Cypriot throne at that time was sent to Constantinople upon the Emperor's invitation. The meetings there weren't exactly revolutionary, but they did spark some small amount of cooperation between the Empire and the Kingdom, with the Emperor Andronikos III recognizing King Hugh IV as the legitimate King of Cyprus and Jerusalem and promising to help supply any soon-to-be crusade, and Hugh IV vowing to protect the Greeks on his island from forced conversions and undue mistreatment, along with a tenuous guarantee to defend the Empire's territorial integrity, doubtlessly to the annoyance of the titular Latin Empress.

And yet again in 1356 relations seemed to be improving between the Latin east and the Orthodox Empire, for the Emperor John V himself was invited to visit the island of Cyprus under the protection of the King Guy II, an invitation that was accepted. In the meeting at the Lusignan capital, an agreement was again reached, with Guy II paying a tribute of coin to the Emperor, in exchange for military aid in taking the Crown of Cilicia. King Guy II also agreed to consult with the Emperor before conquering any lands in Anatolia, thus opening the door to perhaps a more long-term commitment to each other's regional interests. Indeed the partnership seemed to work out perfectly in the beginning, with the Empire providing the troops that, if they did not come to the aid of the Latins, would have made the conflict very difficult, if not completely unwinnable for the Lusignan King. There were growing feelings in the royal court of Cyprus that there was a certain debt now owed to the Empire for just how much aid they gave in the conflict, but those feelings would quickly die out as Cilicia was quickly lost to the Mamluks.

---
In an instant all that had been built up between the Romans and the eastern Latins had come crashing down in the late 1360's. It's true that the Byzantine Emperor had no legal obligation to provide aid to the Lusignans as Cilicia was embroiled in a long and bloody conflict with the Saracens, but to the majority of the nobles in the royal court it still felt like a great betrayal. Their pleas to Constantinople found no welcoming ears, it was as if the gates to the great Imperial city had suddenly and violently been shut before them. Did the Emperor himself not visit them and dine on their food just a few years before? Instead John V set out to help his new Hafsid allies, in a move that was undoubtably the correct strategic choice, but still nevertheless created much resentment. Left with just some limited Genoese and Hospitaller support, the Kingdom of Cilicia soon fell, and unable to blame themselves for the failure, the Frankish nobles knew who they would pin their grievances on...

Thus the Empire and the Lusignan Kingdom had little interactions with each other for the rest of the century. For instance, when the granddaughter of John V was sought a hand in marriage, the Lusignan's pridefully didn't even offer up a distant cousin. Instead, the two states went their own way, with the Lusignan Kingdom becoming dominated by the Genoese after they had defeated and captured a young Peter I in battle, and the Romans consolidating their position in the Balkans and Anatolia. Only in more recent years have relations began to improve between the eastern Latins and Romans, as old grudges began to die with those who held so tightly to them. In a new age, the young did not feel so bound by mistakes of the past.

Most prominently, the Crown Princess Catherine de Lusignan would write a letter to the Imperial Palaiologoi family after the death of the Emperor John V in 1403, despite how much he was still disdained amongst the aging Frankish nobility.

The new Emperor Michael X responded in kind, and for the first time in decades there seemed to be a renewed respect between Cyprus and Byzantium. In fact, it was the Byzantine and Cypriot diplomats working together who sparked the emergence of the Tenth Crusade, which was wildly successful and saw the reestablishment of the crusader presence in the Levant. Still, relations could never be simple between them, even in this newfound cooperation together, and they were certainly not close friends. For although the Byzantines helped start the crusade, they did not provide any coin, armies, or navies to the cause, choosing instead to use their forces to settle a new territorial dispute with the Karamanids, which ultimately ended in failure.

There was doubtlessly some smug satisfaction at that result from some of the old nobility, as the crusaders then went on to reconquer Cilicia, once the place where the Byzantines had supposedly betrayed them. Because of the Byzantine's lack of direct aid to the crusade, there was also no feeling of debt owed to them by the victorious crusaders in the east, busy securing their reconquered Kingdoms. Instead, they elected to stay out of the Byzantine-Karamanid war, and when the Turks emerged triumphant, Queen Catherine I de Lusignan, despite her sympathies for the Empire, still elected to sign the Treaty of Silifke with the Sultan.

This treaty, though not explicitly aimed against the Empire, is still doubtlessly seen as hostile by the Imperial court in Constantinople. Yet, it is also doubtful that Catherine could be blamed for doing so. The Byzantines have, since centuries prior, during even the First Crusade itself, proven themselves unreliable and untrustworthy in regards to the Latin east. They abandoned the First Crusade, chose to help in the defense of Sicily instead of the defense of Cilicia, and have never truly made much of an effort in increasing ties to the Lusignan Kingdoms.

The Treaty of Silifke thus seemed like a godsend to the court of Jerusalem. In an instant the Kingdom of Cilicia and the Duchy of Antioch could be secured from a Turkish invasion, something not even a Byzantine alliance could guarantee, and a strong ally in the region finally acquired to deter renewed Mamluk aggression, something the restored Kingdom of Jerusalem desperately sought. An alliance of a similar sort with the Byzantines seemed almost assured in the times of the early 1360's, but the collapse of the good relations in the decades following thus gave way for the opportunity for another to enter into Jerusalem's good graces.

Yet even still it is unlikely that Queen Catherine genuinely meant to offend the Byzantines by seeking secured borders with the ascendant Turks. It was, like the Byzantines choice to defend the Hafsids instead of the Lusignans, an act of pure strategic necessity. Nevertheless, the Queen has yet to receive any word from the Emperor Thomas I or the Imperial Court expressing their dissatisfaction with the treaty, so perhaps she remains under the naïve assumption that they do not directly object to it, even though the nature of the defensive pact does go against the Byzantine's more expansionist policies in regard to Anatolia.

However, with the Knights Hospitaller returning to the Holy Land and their Order falling under the authority of the Kingdom of Jerusalem oncemore, it is likely that for the first time since the Komnenoi ruled the Empire, the territorial interests of the Crusader States and Byzantium will collide, as the island of Rhodes, quickly being left to the wayside as the Order takes command of Antioch instead, now becomes an attractive target for the Empire.

It is an opportunity, to either bring in a new, long awaited age of cooperation between the Empire and the Crusaders, or to see the relations fall to a never before seen low, perhaps dividing the Christian east for centuries to come...

-# The Leaders of the First Crusade being received by the Emperor Alexios I Komnenos in 1097.

**(End)**

events.author: Rafaam
events.snippet:
**A warning to the Ruler of Cyprus, Kingdom of Jerusalem and Cilicia**

Thomas I Palaiologos has not been in power for long at the moment when he deci
events.fullText:
**A warning to the Ruler of Cyprus, Kingdom of Jerusalem and Cilicia**

Thomas I Palaiologos has not been in power for long at the moment when he decided to write a letter addressed to the Ruler of Cyprus (will not be stating all 3 titles every time, but they are implied). He had only recently learned of the Treaty of Silifke signed between the Turkish Beylik Karaman and the Latin Kingdoms of the East.

Once the news reached his ears, he would give a swift command to his scribes and the Imperial archive in the University of Constantinople to find any pertaining documents that could be relevant in addressing this newly emerged issue. 

The Emperor was reminded of how Byzantine forces nearly alone took the Kingdome of Cilicia for the Cypriot King, at the time when both the Knights of the Island of Rhodes and the Kingdom on the Island could not take the kingdom themselves. 

And that, to his dismay, it was true in the same manner that the at the time Emperor Ioannis V did not support the defense of the Kingdom of Cilicia, instead preferring to support the then newly formed alliance with the Tunisian Sultan. 

Coming closer, when the now Emperor Thomas I was only a young man, he was sent on a diplomatic mission to the French crown. He was ordered by his father Michail X, to persuade the French and their allies to assist Byzantium in a crusade against the Karaman and the Mamluks, as they had shared a military bond, and attacking on one, would equal attacking both the Bey and the Sultan. 

The French <@460814348487753730>  listened, and indeed rallied the troops to master a crusading host after the insisting pleas from the future Emperor. But as the news of the crusade came to the East, Karaman <@1216097200401154181>  was afraid to be destroyed in such a scenario, and in their fear, betrayed the Mamluks of Egypt.

At this moment Byzantium was placed before a choice, support the Crusaders who had promised no reward to the Byzantines in case of the success of the Crusade, or use this opportunity to strike the Karamanids. The second option was chosen by Michail X, and the Emperor would waste the rest of his life fighting that fruitless war. 

Which leads to the current moment and the dilemma that lays before Thomas. In this moment, the Scribes had once again returned in the chambers of the Emperor, with the oldest documents. Of them, was the [Declaration](https://discord.com/channels/507915886477312023/1505545492736639056/1507480456843497623) made by the Emperor Andronikos IV, in the last years of his life as well as the following [Treaty of Hierapolis](https://discord.com/channels/507915886477312023/1508990612345720852/1510097286200430802) signed by the Emperor Ioannis V and the Crusader Order of the Knights Hospitaller with the direct sanction of the Holy See. 

Both these documents had given the Empire full grounds to perceive the Conquest of concerned lands - as aggression towards the Empire. 

"Any ruler, army, mercenary company, or foreign power that deliberately seeks to obstruct the restoration of these lands to the Empire shall henceforth be regarded as a blood enemy of the Roman State."

Had read Thomas the document laying before him. The Emperor understood that to forget this promise is to make the Empire look weak in the eyes of those around it. And he knew that this could not be let to pass. But he also knew that he could not afford to throw threats around.

As such, he would write a letter:

||To the Ruler of Cyprus, Monarch of the Kingdoms of Cyprus, Jerusalem and Cilicia, guardian of the Holy Land and the Friend of the Empire <@698400527834415145> 

I write you in spirit of trust and understanding, adding the scribed copies of the documents that i have found in our Imperial archives. My grand-grand father had made a promise, and i intend on keeping that promise, just the same that my line had kept all the promises made before. We had helped your Crown come to Cilicia, and were willing to guide the busy crown of France to the Crusade upon the Holy lands. 

It is upon my honor to finish the reclamation started by Andronikos IV and execute the contract made with the Knights Hospitaller. I understand the necessity of your recent relations with the Beylik of Karaman, even though i do not condone them, we must do what we must do, to preserve ourselves. We, Romans, had understood this very well in the recent century.

Yet my personal understanding does not change my obligation to my people and to my ancestors. The Western Anatolia belongs to the Empire, and so, i must warn you. Should you attempt to stop the Empire from returning that land into our control - my hand will be forced. This is not a threat, but a warning. Denying these claims, supported by the Holy See, in favor of an alliance with the Savages, is the same as giving up your Christian nature. 

I only hope that you find my words reasonable, and we can still find common ground for future relations.

(OOC: You may still uphold your alliance with Karamanids, and you may even fight for them in a war. But you are not to obstruct Byzantium from taking the lands that were proclaimed in the Reclamation of Western Anatolia and the Treaty of Hierapolis. If you obstruct conquest of those particular lands - you will fall under the conditions of the Reclamation and will become a blood enemy of the Empire.)||

events.author: Rafaam
events.snippet:
**A letter from Eagle to Eagle**

To His Majesty Silvester Nemanjic, King of Serbia,

From Thomas I Palaiologos, Emperor and Autocrat of the Romans, D
events.fullText:
**A letter from Eagle to Eagle**

To His Majesty Silvester Nemanjic, King of Serbia,

From Thomas I Palaiologos, Emperor and Autocrat of the Romans, Defender of Constantinople and Guardian of the Eastern Empire.

||The world around us has changed more swiftly than many had foreseen. While Hungarian influence penetrates deeper into lands that once belonged to proud and sovereign rulers. Bulgaria, once counted among the great kingdoms of our region, has all but vanished from the maps of Europe.

It is in such times that we must make decisions otherwise unthinkable to a ruler of our time.

For this reason, We extend to You an offer that no Serbian ruler has ever before received.

Should Your Majesty choose to bend the knee before the lawful Emperor of the Roman Empire and enter into Our protection as a loyal vassal and ally, We shall bestow upon the Royal House of Serbia the hereditary dignity of Vicar of the Balkans.

From the moment of its proclamation, the Roman Empire shall renounce all direct territorial expansion beyond its present Balkan frontiers for as long as this agreement endures. Furthermore, no Emperor of Rome shall possess the lawful authority to revoke this covenant so long as the Kings of Serbia faithfully retain their office as Vicars of the Balkans.

Any lands acquired within the Balkans, whether by conquest, diplomacy, inheritance, or any other means shall be recognized by Constantinople as the rightful domain of the Serbian Crown, to govern with full autonomy beneath the dignity of its Imperial office.

In return, the Kingdom of Serbia shall stand as the representative of Imperial interests throughout the Balkans.

We offer that Serbia become the hand through which the Roman Empire preserves peace throughout the Balkans, while the Empire itself turns its strength toward securing the seas, Anatolia, and the restoration of the Greatness of the Empire of the Romans.||

<@759277551117402194>

(OOC: The agreement to this will not make you my vassal immediately, it will just happen once we can naturally get you from the hands of Hungary, maybe during this crisis, maybe later)

events.author: Cod In A Pod

**Formation of the Hansyhorod Jewish District**

For decades, now, the town of Hansyhorod has slowly prosperered alongside the major trading routes an
events.fullText:
**Formation of the Hansyhorod Jewish District**

For decades, now, the town of Hansyhorod has slowly prosperered alongside the major trading routes and other towns in the area. Ever since the foundation late in the 1390s', the town has slowly grown into a vital outpost for the Germanic traders, and then, settlers and artisans, who have come to practice trades in the region with lower competition and higher demand. The town has seen huge growth in the beginning of the 15th century, with a great migration of people in years of 1404-1408, forming what has become the Germanic centre of the town, establishing many small shops, operated exclusively by Hanseatic settlers. In those years, the Catholic Church has finally begun operating at a decent capacity, finally fulfilling the needs of thousands of Catholics in the Kingdom by that point.

With the inflow of the Germans, however, came another group - Jewish refugees from Bohemia. Fleeing the most recent spike in antisemitism, they have clung to the story of the town, free of religious persecution. While along the march many have stayed in the already promising Poland, a good portion of those that made it ended up settling in the town.

While the story was legally true and the Jews were, in fact, not directly approached by either Catholic or Orthodox Churches, following Queen Euphemia's decision to allow them to stay, they have, nonetheless, been separated by their German counterparts. Yet, despite the fairly open hostility from the Germans, they have banded together and formed a separate district of the town.

Practicing first and foremost jewelry, as the trade has seen huge boom since the deeper integration of Volhynia and Hungarian Carpathians, the jews became an integral part of the local economy and life, also beginning to engage in other artisinal crafts and agriculture outside of the town, as well, as trade, the Jews seem to have settled.

events.author: ST Peter
events.snippet:
# The Quest for the Holy Grail.

With the successful conclusion of the Tenth Crusade, not only did Jerusalem find itself in Catholic hands oncemore, b
events.fullText:
# The Quest for the Holy Grail.

With the successful conclusion of the Tenth Crusade, not only did Jerusalem find itself in Catholic hands oncemore, but so too did parts of the old Principality of Antioch. This reconquest allowed a band of 12 devout and Holy knights to resume the quest they had been embarking on for over 4 decades, the search for the **Holy Grail**. This legendary Holy artifact had been rumored to exist for centuries, and bands of Crusading Knights had tried again and again to find its whereabouts throughout the centuries of Catholic presence in the Levant. It was unknown if any at all succeeded. The twelve knights, calling themselves the "Knights of the Holy Grail", spent decades researching all they could about the Holy Grail and where it may be located, so that its Holy properties, which grant the drinker immortality, could be used for the betterment of all Christendom. Eventually, their quest brought them to investigate the journey of three Knights, all of them brothers, during the First Crusade.

After finding 2 of the long-dead brothers, all clues suddenly led to Alexandretta and the Canyon of the Crescent Moon, but the Knights of the Holy Grail could not confirm what their decades of research concluded due to the Christians having lost the city over a century ago. The Mamluks certainly weren't going to allow a band of roaming Knights potentially find an artifact that would undoubtedly legitimize the Christian faith. The Knights despaired that they great Holy quest was to end in failure...until the Tenth Crusade saw Alexandretta fall back it Christian hands. Overtaken with jubilation, the Knights eagerly set out to Alexandretta, and after much searching they finally found the hidden Temple of the Sun, carved into the Canyon of the Crescent Moon. It's entrance was only wide enough for one man at a time to enter, and so the aging Knights did so, one after another.

At the great seal in the entrance of the temple, the twelve Knights of the Holy Grail prayed openly, thanking God for allowing them to find such a sacred place. Yet, their trials were only just beginning, for there was a series of challenges meant to test one's faith in God before they could be allowed to find the Grail. The first, the **"Breath of God"**, took the lives of 8 of the Knights, for it took until Sir Raymond to figure out that when one felt the wind coming from down the catacombs, one must kneel before this "breath of God", to avoid being decapitated by two huge circular blades.

The next trial was the **"Word of God"**. There were letters all along the floor, and the four remaining Knights knew what was to be spelt. "Jéhovah", unfortunately for the French speakers however, they misunderstood that this trial was built with the Latin spelling in mind, in which Jéhovah is actually spelt "Iehova". This misunderstanding caused one of the Knights to step onto the wrong letter, in a panic he grabbed onto the knight behind him for support, unwittingly sending the both of them falling down a dark pit with seemingly no end. The remaining two Knights, including Sir Raymond, successfully traversed the trial after seeing their companions fall.

The third trial was to be the **"Path of God"**. There was an endless canyon...and no way to get across. Sir Raymond knew what they had to do...they had to take a leap of faith. He tried to convince his sole remaining companion to accompany him in the leap, but the other Knight, having seen the rest of his Knightly brothers die, panicked and refused, quickly abandoning Sir Raymond and leaving the temple. Later in life, this Knight was brought to such shame by his cowardice and lack of faith in God that he would never speak of the temple or the trials ever again...

Alone except for God, Sir Raymond made the Sign of the Cross, and with a deep breath took a step towards the bottomless canyon. To his relief, he somehow stepped on solid ground, despite the fact that it seemed like nothing was there! The Knight would profusely thank God, before ultimately moving on...

And in the next room, he would see the faint glow of burning fires, and the shadow of a man sat, his backing facing Sir Raymond. Raymond would step towards this figure, when he noticed that the man before him also seemed to be a Knight! In an instant however, the aging knight would stand and turn and swing his sword at Sir Raymond. Raymond would draw his own sword and a short duel would ensue, until Raymond would disarm his opponent. Out of breath and seeking answers, he would suddenly notice the satisfaction on the old Knight's face, and he would speak to Raymond with a warm smile and a gentle voice:

** "Ah, I knew you would come. My strength is leaving me."**

Raymond would ask who he was, to which he would respond:

**"The last of three brothers, who swore an oath to find the Grail...and to guard it."**

Sir Raymond's eyes would widen as the realization of who this was finally hit him. He was one of the three brothers that Raymond's Knightly band had been following the journey of. But, if that were true...that would make this barely aged Knight over three centuries old! Raymond would exclaim in shock:

** "You came here during the First Crusade...that was three hundred years ago."**

With a soft chuckle, the aging Knight would reply:

** "Yes...a long time to wait. I was chosen, because I was the bravest...the most worthy. The honor was mine, until another came to challenge me to single combat, and pass the duty...to you, who vanquished me."**

The chosen Knight would offer up his sword to Sir Raymond, in an offer to take his place.

https://tenor.com/view/i-knew-you-would-come-indiana-jones-knight-hello-darkness-welcome-gif-16996507

Yet Raymond shook his head, and graciously refused the Knight's sword, saying kindly:

** "No Sir, I cannot do this. I cannot slay one of God's chosen..."**

The aging Knight would give a small, reluctant smile, almost as if he wanted to be vanquished, and finally relieved of his long duty of guarding the Grail. In this small moment of silence, Sir Raymond looked around the small room. Along the back and side walls there were dozens upon dozens of Grails, all of them beautiful and resplendent in their glory. There was also the lone book that the centuries-old Knight had to fill his time with, it was a humble Bible. Raymond was taken out of his thoughts by a question that was now lingering in the back of his mind:

**"So...which one is it? The Holy Grail..."**

The warm expression on the aging Knight's face dropped, and his voice suddenly had a much more heavy weight to it as he replied:

** "You must choose which Grail is the one of God. But choose wisely, for as the true Grail will bring you life, a false Grail will take it from you..."**

Sir Raymond would give a slight nod, before walking amongst the many rows of Grails. There...there were so many, all so beautiful and worthy of God. Until finally, one caught his eye. It was stunning, gold plated, with jewels adorning its entire crest. Truly the splendor of God was shown here in this Grail. Raymond would let out a whisper:

** "It's more beautiful than I'd ever imagined..."**

Solemnly, Sir Raymond would carry this exquisite Grail to the nearby basin of water, and he would scoop some into his Grail. He would make one more Sign of the Cross, before chugging down several mouthfuls of the Holy Water. For a few seconds, Raymond thought he felt immortality surge through his body, before he suddenly and violently collapsed to his knees in agony. In an instant he found himself unable to breathe, his grail clanging to the ground, spilling out what water remaining in it.

Sir Raymond would look up at the centuries-old Knight, desperate for answers as he felt his life slipping away from him. He had never seen the Knight so somber. Perhaps he was sad for Raymond's imminent fate, perhaps he was sad for himself that no one would now be able to take over his duties now...or perhaps it was both. In any case, with his voice somber and sympathetic, his expression displaying nothing but remorse, he would reluctantly say:

** "I'm so sorry..."**

After another moment, Raymond would collapse fully to the ground as he felt himself close his eyes forever, with the aging Knight saying lastly:

**"...you chose...*poorly*."**

Raymond then died there, in the very room where the real Holy Grail lay. His body would soon turn to dust, leaving only his armor to mark where he had met his end. The aging Knight, sad at the loss of a faithful, if not worthy, Knight, returned to his duties, and opened the Bible oncemore to continue his service to God. As it turned out, he would need to wait another half-millennium for another Knight to come, to finally find one worthy of taking his place. Though that Knight wouldn't be at all what he expected, with a strange hat, and a whip at his side instead of a sword. Until then, the aging Knight would continue to do his duty, and he knew that God would do the rest...

-# (Left to Right) The Aging Knight reading his Bible in the chamber of the Holy Grail, The Aging Knight giving his farewells to the Last Crusaders in the year 1938.

**(End)**

events.author: JPThunda
events.snippet:
**The Honorable Kingdom of Castile**
 

Following the declaration of by the Sultan, and the subsequent decree by the Kingdom of Castile, a letter was
events.fullText:
**The Honorable Kingdom of Castile**
 

Following the declaration of by the Sultan, and the subsequent decree by the Kingdom of Castile, a letter was delivered to the Castilian Kingdom.

||*To the Noble Fadrique de Trastamara, King of Castile, King of Leon, & King of Navarra,*

*Your actions to curtail Catholic piracy within the Mediterranean are a credit to you and your people.  My father spoke well of the Castilian people, and I have seen for myself your conduct in matters of peace and war.  I commend you on your unbesmirched and honorable reputation.*

*It is for these reasons that I have commanded my fleets to ensure that no risk of harm comes to the Castilian lands as a result of our hunt for the Pirates of Visby, whom the Pope of Lyon names ‘Holy Order’.  This shall include the lands of your wife, the Queen Catherine of Lusignan, in the levant and upon the island of Cyprus.*

*In light of this, I ask for a guarantee of safe conduct for those clerics and pilgrims who wish to visit the holy places peaceably.  In return, I shall instruct my fleets to ensure the safe conduct of all those who travel to the Holy Land by sea upon vessels of Castilian or Jerusalemite provenance.  I hope you shall find these terms agreeable.*

*~Sultan Bahir Hafsid, Sultan of Carthage, Sheik of Algiers, King of Trinacria, Sayk of Zab, Protector of Sardinia*|| 

<@698400527834415145> <@422498641425596418>

**Elpido Abate elected Pope <:mary3:1126382749612249108> **
-# https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EyqDPakdBAA

"By the Holy Spirit, come to the Hou
events.fullText:
****

**Elpido Abate elected Pope <:mary3:1126382749612249108> **
-# https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EyqDPakdBAA

"By the Holy Spirit, come to the House of God and behold yourself to the **corruption of men**<:france_:842523808028033045> . And yet, men of Hus abandon her, **but true men of God pick up the pieces to restore what was lost." <:mary3:1126382749612249108> **

**This is a victory of the Holy Mother Church, to restore the honour that has been lost.**

**God has tested Christendom** on many fronts, and by the Age of Strife, now comes the promised uplift. The final hammer has fallen, and the conclave has met and agreed that the time for justice, honour, and the glory of Christ <:AveChristusRex:836370696904114186> be restored. Elpido Abate promised the restoration of the Roman Church, and he shall do so. In the face of such evil as the Hussites, the Church has chosen to elect the traditional candidate, **endorsed by the Orsini House**<:rose:865579087883272192> , to reclaim the Pax Orsini that had been lost to the wiles **of unworthy men! <:france:1209832243896524830> **

As such Elpido Abate shall do the following in his first year of taking office:

1. **He shall move back to Rome** <:romerome2:899964960506003477> , where the Pope shall sit, firm in the Roman Church, surrounded by the Italian Cardinals who have run the Church for centuries, without the influence of the French Cardinals interfering in his day-to-day operations of the Church.

2. He shall formally announce the accession of the Visby Order <@326414282851876875> for their righteous and correct actions against the heathens! They shall be tasked with the reclamation of the Mediterranean Islands, which shall be granted to the new Crusader Order under the glory of Christ and the Holy Mother Church! <:crusader:1082041113428381706> 

3. Jerusalem shall become protected by the Holy Mother Church <:RoyaumedeJerusalem:843460314276167690>  and shall be given a **Cardinal of Jerusalem**, who shall represent Christs return to the Holy Land! The Crusades have succeeded, and now this signals the golden age of Christendom! **Rejoice!**

**4. Hussitism is declared banned, and every church, monastery, cathedral, parish, and school is to ban the discussion of Hussitism, as it is a direct heresy, an abandonment of the Holy Mother Church, and a clear deviation by Satan. Any Hussite books are to be burned, and the crime of even speaking his name shall be punished by three lashings per word mentioned.<:Discipline:1196712836622205048> <:Discipline:1196712836622205048> <:Discipline:1196712836622205048> <:Discipline:1196712836622205048> <:Discipline:1196712836622205048> <:Discipline:1196712836622205048> <:Discipline:1196712836622205048> **


**Elpido Abate is openly and unashamedly an Orsini Pope**<:rose:865579087883272192> , and the Orsini will proudly take the mantle of Curia Controller to show Christendom that the Church can and will be restored through the glory of **Pax Orsini. **Gold and tributes to the cause are a sign of your gladness, **and indulgences shall not be apologised for.** Give up your wealth to the Church, which shall use it in the progression of the Crusaders aims ||and, of course, the new bath houses... <:Beer:1514296080865558588> ||

events.author: JPThunda
events.snippet:
**The Corsair Wars**
 

As the armies and fleets of Carthage make ready to prosecute their war against the Papal-backed pirates and brigands of the Vi
events.fullText:
**The Corsair Wars**
 

As the armies and fleets of Carthage make ready to prosecute their war against the Papal-backed pirates and brigands of the Visby ‘Holy Order’, a letter of demand is delivered to Malta, to the headquarters of the Order of the Knights of the Hospital of St. John of Jerusalem.  A war fleet of no less than 20 carracks and 5 escort galleys conveys the letter, and it is handed to the castellan of the fortress at Rhodes by an aged Sicilian man, though age has done little to dull his wits or the sharpness in his eyes.
.

*Grandmaster Simon Simeon of the Knights Hospitaller,*

*It is the will of the Sultan Bahir Hafsid, Peace be on his Name, Sultan of Carthage, Sheik of Algiers, King of Trinacria, Sayk of Zab, and Protector of Sardinia, that the piracy inflicted upon the Mediterranean at the hands of the Catholic Holy Orders, at the direction of the Pope in Lyon, shall cease, and that the Holy Orders shall be removed from the Mediterranean Sea henceforth and forthwith.*

*By the command of my Sultan, at whose pleasure does commerce flow, and by whose will our mighty fleet sails, you are thus commanded to depart these seas and never return.  Your lands and the civilian people who dwell upon it, along with those who remove their tabards and lay down their arms, shall be conveyed to the Roman Emperor Thomas Palaiologos to act as custodian in accordance with the treaty between his august Empire and your Order.  Else, if you will not depart peaceably, then you are commanded to foreswear all allegiance to and support of the Pope of Thieves in Lyon, and to swear before God, on pain of death, to never raise the black flag against any who sail the Mediterranean, be they Christian or Muslim.*

*It is only by the mercy of Queen Eirini Palaiologos, wife of the Sultan, that this offer is made to you, for others who have refused my Sultan’s commands and instead act in consort with the Pope of Thieves shall receive no such mercy.  Your reply is expected within the year, else your refusal of terms shall be assumed.*

*So Delivered by the hand of Grand Admiral Pagano da Val di Girgenti of Carthage*

<@698400527834415145> <@302128861209165824>

events.author: Cloud
events.snippet:
### Request for the return of the City of Lyon
King Charles V de Valois of France formally requests the return of the City of Lyon with Elpido Abate d
events.fullText:
### Request for the return of the City of Lyon
King Charles V de Valois of France formally requests the return of the City of Lyon with Elpido Abate decision to move the  Papacy back to Rome. A de jure city of the French Crown lended to the Lyonese Popes since the start of Western Schism and so after, now no longer need be lent to the Papacy with the Pope's return to Roma. 

As sign of good faith King Charles V in return promises the [continuance of allowing the Holy Knights of Visby to dock in France’s Mediterranean ports](https://discordapp.com/channels/507915886477312023/1505545331591610549/1520276853103788044), [guaranteeing the sovereignty of the Kingdom of Jerusalem](https://discordapp.com/channels/507915886477312023/1505545415213449216/1520270026085503106), and even offering to lend his humble ~~outdated~~ fleet in combating Carthaginian aggression in Mediterranean.

events.fullText:
**The Expanded Rights of Ruhr**
Due to the ever growing sister county of Ruhr's growth it has become more apparent that Ruhr has grown to a partner and will be given the following rights.

1. The County of Ruhr is now a equal partner and shall be treated as such (Break of Vassalage)

2. The Duke no longer claims the land as his own land instead it is own by him thru claim to Ruhr itself (Break of Fiefdom)

3. The Duke no longer cannot take money unless the nobles of Ruhr can agree on such issues (Freedom of Treasury) 

4. The uniting of the lands of Berg and Ruhr to the Limbourgs to bring the to Duchy and County to prosperity (Union Created)

5. We recognize that Ruhr can make its own ties and will not go in between them unless it puts both realms in danger (Recognition of Sovereignty)
